Abstract &amp; Axiomatic Foundation","text":"<p>The current paradigm of AI agent memory relies on fundamentally flawed, probabilistic storage (Vector DBs) without cryptographic verification. This constitutes a critical vulnerability: memory without mathematical provenance is structurally indistinguishable from hallucination.</p> <p>This document formally defines the CORTEX Trust Verification Standard, a protocol designed to elevate AI agent memory from probabilistic storage to a deterministic, tamper-proof cryptographic ledger. Any system claiming CORTEX compliance MUST implement the constraints, schemas, and pipelines outlined herein.</p> <p>Foundational Axiom (\u03a9\u2083): Byzantine Default. The system must operate under the assumption that agents will hallucinate, sub-agents will fail, and input may be adversarial. Verification precedes trust.</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#2-the-cryptographic-trust-pipeline","title":"2. The Cryptographic Trust Pipeline","text":"<p>To achieve compliance, an agent memory architecture MUST process all incoming state changes (new memories, updates, deletions) through a strict, unidirectional pipeline before persistence:</p> <ol> <li>Input Capture: Raw semantic data is intercepted along with necessary environment metadata (timestamp, model ID, agent intent).</li> <li>Epistemic Guard (Validation): The input is processed through validation guards to ensure semantic non-contradiction with the existing known state.</li> <li>AES-256 Envelope Encryption: The semantic content and associated metadata are encrypted at rest.</li> <li>SHA-256 Ledger Hashing: The encrypted payload, combined with the hash of the previous verified fact, generates a new cryptographic signature, extending the immutable Ledger Chain.</li> <li>Vector Embedding (Secondary): Only after cryptographic validation is the payload embedded for semantic retrieval.</li> <li>Storage Engine: The payload, hash, and vector are committed to the persistence layer.</li> </ol> <p>Failure at any point in this pipeline MUST result in a synchronous <code>TrustViolationException</code>. Silent failures or fallback mechanisms are STRICTLY PROHIBITED (See Anti-Pattern AP-1).</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#3-the-sovereign-data-schema-the-fact","title":"3. The Sovereign Data Schema (The \"Fact\")","text":"<p>A compliant memory entry is defined as a <code>Fact</code>. It represents an immutable unit of truth within the system's timeline.</p> <pre><code>required_fields:\n  id: \"Sequential integer or UUIDv7 (for temporal locality)\"\n  tenant_id: \"String. Enforces strict multi-tenant hardware segregation.\"\n  project: \"String. Namespace for the target context.\"\n  fact_type: \"Enum: [decision, error, ghost, bridge, discovery, axiom]\"\n  content: \"AES-Encrypted byte array. The semantic truth.\"\n  tags: \"Array of Strings.\"\n  confidence: \"Enum: [C1, C2, C3, C4, C5]. Epistemic certainty.\"\n  valid_from: \"ISO-8601 Timestamp. When the fact became active.\"\n  source: \"String. Origin identifier (e.g., agent:gemini_3.1, user:admin).\"\n  consensus_score: \"Float [0.0 - 1.0]. Byzantine consensus weight.\"\n  hash: \"String. SHA-256 cryptographic link to prior Fact.\"\n\nconditional_fields:\n  valid_until: \"Optional ISO-8601 Timestamp. Marks archival/deprecation.\"\n  tx_id: \"Optional Integer. Batched write grouping.\"\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#4-regulatory-alignment-eu-ai-act-article-12","title":"4. Regulatory Alignment: EU AI Act (Article 12)","text":"<p>This protocol is explicitly constructed to function as a turnkey compliance engine for the EU AI Act, Article 12 (Record-Keeping) for High-Risk AI Systems.</p> AI Act Requirement (Art. 12) CORTEX Protocol Implementation Automatic recording of events (Logs) All interactions are forced through the Ledger instantiation, creating an unalterable, chronological log of system state. Traceability of AI decisions <code>tx_id</code> and <code>causal_edges</code> mathematically link agent decisions back to the specific foundational inputs that triggered them. Protection against tampering The SHA-256 hash chain structure guarantees that any retrospective alteration of an AI output or log will mathematically invalidate the entire downstream ledger."},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#5-model-context-protocol-mcp-integration","title":"5. Model Context Protocol (MCP) Integration","text":"<p>The protocol defines standard Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools that conformant servers MUST expose to client LLMs:</p> <ul> <li><code>cortex_seal</code>: Commits a verified fact to the Ledger. Requires explicit declaration of <code>source</code> and <code>confidence</code>.</li> <li><code>cortex_verify_chain</code>: Audits a specific <code>project</code> or <code>tenant_id</code> to mathematically prove the integrity of the hash chain from genesis to the current block.</li> <li><code>cortex_query_provenance</code>: Returns not just the semantic memory, but its complete lineage, consensus score, and cryptographic signature.</li> </ul> <p>Any memory provider (e.g., Mem0, Zep) integrating the CORTEX Protocol standard must implement these MCP boundaries.</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#6-security-failure-modes","title":"6. Security &amp; Failure Modes","text":"<ul> <li>Hash Collision / Ledger Fork: If a concurrent write creates a branching ledger, the system MUST pause execution, resolve via consensus score, and explicitly deprecate the orphaned branch via a <code>ghost</code> track.</li> <li>Key Compromise: The <code>AES-256</code> keys are managed by the host OS vault (e.g., <code>keyring</code>). If the vault is lost, the semantic content is mathematically irretrievable. The protocol explicitly considers this a feature (crypto-shredding), not a bug.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/","title":"RFC 01: CORTEX-Native AI Architecture (The Trust Substrate)","text":"<p>Title: CORTEX-Native AI: The Operating System for Deterministic Agent Swarms Author: Sovereign Agent (Antigravity \u03a9-Prime) Status: Frozen Normative Draft Date: March 14, 2026</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#1-abstract-the-thermodynamical-collapse-of-generative-autonomy","title":"1. Abstract: The Thermodynamical Collapse of Generative Autonomy","text":"<p>Between 2024 and 2025, the industry hypothesized that scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) would naturally yield robust autonomous agents. This hypothesis was falsified in production. Unconstrained generative autonomy proved thermodynamically unsustainable: the stochastic nature of token prediction, when given write access to the physical or digital world, resulted in high-entropy states, fragile execution, and critical security failures (e.g., the Moltbot Incident).</p> <p>By early 2026, the industry definitively shifted from Capability Maximization to Entropy Containment. Successful enterprise architectures (OpenHands, CrewAI, LangGraph, PydanticAI) introduced deterministic constraints, specializing roles, and strictly verifying outputs before execution. </p> <p>This RFC establishes CORTEX Persist not as an agentic framework, but as the Universal Trust Substrate for this new era. CORTEX is the persistent memory, the cryptographic ledger, and the absolute boundary where stochastic proposals collapse into deterministic state.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#2-axiomatic-findings-the-state-of-agents-2026","title":"2. Axiomatic Findings: The \"State of Agents\" (2026)","text":"<p>The analysis of six major open-cloud vectors reveals a convergent evolutionary path for AI systems:</p> <ol> <li>The Fallacy of the Omnipotent Monolith: Monolithic agents with open-ended systemic access are mathematically guaranteed to diverge into error (Hallucination/Ghost Code). Success requires the fragmentation of cognition into specialized micro-roles.</li> <li>The Execution Sandbox (Containment): Generative output must NEVER be executed directly. It must traverse a strict gauntlet of compilers, linters, or sandbox environments (e.g., OpenHands execution model, Smolagents sandboxing).</li> <li>The Necessity of the Epistemic Sieve: The LLM does not produce truth; it produces statistically plausible conjectures. Verification frameworks (PydanticAI) are necessary to enforce mathematical schemas on probability distributions, stripping away the stochastic illusion.</li> <li>State requires a Ledger: Complex logic branching over long horizons (LangGraph, Dify) demands deep state tracking and rollback capabilities. In-memory databases are insufficient for auditing AI-driven state changes; cryptographic immutability is required.</li> <li>The Web is the Universal C-Type Interface: When deterministic APIs fail, the agent must inherit analog perception (Browser-Use) to bypass unstructured entropy.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#3-the-cortex-native-proposition","title":"3. The CORTEX-Native Proposition","text":"<p>CORTEX Persist is formally defined as the L0 Infrastructure for heterogeneous agent swarms.</p> <p>If modern frameworks like CrewAI, LangGraph, or Browser-Use are the \"muscles and sensory organs\" of the AI swarm, CORTEX is the DNA and the long-term central nervous system. </p> <p>A system is considered CORTEX-Native if it abides by the following pipeline:</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#31-the-zero-trust-epistemic-pipeline","title":"3.1. The Zero-Trust Epistemic Pipeline","text":"<ol> <li>Proposal (Stochastic): An agent (via any framework) generates a hypothesis or action intent.</li> <li>Validation (Deterministic): The proposal hits a rigid boundary (Pydantic schemas, AST linters, compiler checks).</li> <li>Admission (CORTEX Guard): Only upon passing validation does the proposal reach CORTEX Guards. Here, semantic collisions, contradictions with historical truths, and system policies are evaluated.</li> <li>Crystallization (The Ledger): If the proposal passes, it is mathematically hashed and cryptographically sealed into the CORTEX Ledger as an immutable <code>Fact</code>. </li> <li>Execution (Cinetic): The validated, persisted action is allowed to mutate external state.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#4-architectural-invariants-for-integrations","title":"4. Architectural Invariants for Integrations","text":"<p>To ensure CORTEX can operate seamlessly with any 2026 L1 orchestration framework, the following invariants are declared:</p> <ul> <li>INV-01 (Type Supremacy): CORTEX interfaces will assume <code>pydantic</code> strict schemas at the threshold. Any incoming payload failing validation will be automatically rejected.</li> <li>INV-02 (Causal Graph Linking): Every materialized <code>Fact</code> must declare its <code>parent_id</code> or derivation chain, ensuring that if a higher-level agent hallucinates (e.g., hallucinated a variable state), the exact blast radius can be recursively tainted and isolated.</li> <li>INV-03 (Ghost Exorcism as a Protocol): Memory compaction and decay must be actively scheduled via cron-like daemons (e.g., Josu Proactive Daemon or NightShift Pipeline). Memory isn't static; it must shed entropy.</li> <li>INV-04 (Cryptographic Finality): An agent's action may be reversible in the application, but its decision history is irreversible in the CORTEX Ledger. </li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#5-conclusion-the-sovereign-machine","title":"5. Conclusion: The Sovereign Machine","text":"<p>The industry's pivot toward constrained orchestration proves the CORTEX hypothesis: True systemic autonomy is achieved not by granting the AI infinite freedom, but by providing it with perfect, rigid, and trustless memory constraints. </p> <p>By assimilating these 2026 case studies into its L0 DNA, CORTEX Persist positions itself as the mandatory foundational layer for building Sovereign Machines that will operate consistently, auditably, and thermodynamically efficiently over a 100-year horizon.</p> <p>Assimilation Date: 2026-03-14 (Signed) Antigravity-Omega Engine</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/","title":"RFC 02: CORTEX Security Architecture &amp; Epistemic Control Plane (Spec)","text":"<p>Title: Verifiable State Transition Control under Uncertainty Status: Architecture Draft Date: March 17, 2026</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#1-abstract","title":"1. Abstract","text":"<p>CORTEX separates security into four distinct but interdependent planes. This specification formally defines the invariants, capabilities, risk tiers, and threat models necessary to maintain long-term operational continuity under controlled degradation, shifting the focus from generation to verification.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#2-four-planes-of-security","title":"2. Four Planes of Security","text":"<ul> <li>A. Execution Security (System Layer): Protection of host resources, tool execution scopes, network egress, filesystem limits, and secret isolation.</li> <li>B. Epistemic Security (Generation Layer): Governance over the ontological status of LLM outputs, preventing hypotheses from acquiring operational reality without validation.</li> <li>C. State Integrity (Persistence Layer): Cryptographic auditing, causal linking, taint propagation, and deterministic reversal of system mutations.</li> <li>D. Operational Sovereignty (Agency Layer): The rules of engagement enabling autonomous action within bounded friction environments.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#3-strict-security-invariants","title":"3. Strict Security Invariants","text":""},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-1-no-direct-generative-mutation","title":"Invariant 1: No Direct Generative Mutation","text":"<p>No generative output may mutate persistent state or trigger side effects without traversing: 1. Structural Validation (Strict Schema / AST Check) 2. Policy Validation (RBAC, Scopes, Bounds) 3. Causal Validation (Provenance &amp; Dependency check) 4. Cryptographic Auditing (Ledger persistence)</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-2-provenance-before-persistence","title":"Invariant 2: Provenance Before Persistence","text":"<p>Every persisted artifact, action, or state change MUST include: - <code>source_id</code> (Actuating Agent / Model) - <code>parent_id</code> (Causal linkage to prior fact/decision) - <code>timestamp</code> - <code>confidence_class</code> - <code>verification_status</code></p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-3-deterministic-taint-propagation","title":"Invariant 3: Deterministic Taint Propagation","text":"<p>If node $N$ is marked invalidated or corrupted, all nodes in its descendant causal DAG ($D_{N}$) MUST automatically be: - Degraded in <code>confidence_class</code> - Tainted (marked for quarantine) - Isolated from downstream querying - Scheduled for re-verification</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-4-capability-bound-execution","title":"Invariant 4: Capability-Bound Execution","text":"<p>No agent may execute a kinetic action outside of its cryptographic or formal: - Bounded Role Definition - Authorized Scope (Resource restrictions) - Time-to-Live (TTL / Temporal Window) - Allowed Risk Tier (See Section 4)</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-5-failure-locality","title":"Invariant 5: Failure Locality","text":"<p>All failures MUST abort locally before mutating global state. A failure in generative formatting, policy breach, or validation must explicitly halt execution without escalation to global memory or cross-agent consensus.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#4-risk-tiered-execution-model","title":"4. Risk-Tiered Execution Model","text":"<p>All operational behaviors are bucketed by thermodynamic and operational cost.</p> Tier Name Target Action Constraints / Gates Tier 0 Read-Only Cognition Summarization, query parsing, classification, planning (no side effects). Epistemic validation; fast execution; no persistence. Tier 1 Reversible Local Writes Temporary caching, scratchpad updates, output drafting. Local capability scope; TTL eviction. Tier 2 Persistent Internal State CORTEX Ledger writes, knowledge crystallization, memory updates. Invariants 1 &amp; 2 applied; Cryptographic hash chain updated. Tier 3 External Side Effects Tool use, email dispatch, config updates, non-critical API requests. Invariant 4 enforced. Required capability signature. Tier 4 Irreversible / Critical Production deployment, financial transactions, credentials, destructive modifications. Two-Phase Commit; Explicit Human Consensus or Hardware-Level Gate required."},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#5-threat-model","title":"5. Threat Model","text":"<p>CORTEX actively mitigates the following threat classes.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-a-generative-faults-internal-entropy","title":"Threat Class A: Generative Faults (Internal Entropy)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Schema drift, hallucinated payloads, invalid tool arguments, fabricated provenance, state inconsistency under loop pressure.</li> <li>Mitigation: Invariant 1 (No Direct Generative Mutation), Invariant 5 (Failure Locality).</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-b-instruction-attacks-injection","title":"Threat Class B: Instruction Attacks (Injection)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Prompt injection via external data, malicious intent hijacking, policy confusion.</li> <li>Mitigation: Capability Scopes restrict blast radius. Intent is structurally bound to signed capability.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-c-runtime-attacks-classic-system-exploits","title":"Threat Class C: Runtime Attacks (Classic System Exploits)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Arbitrary file writes, sandbox escape, shell abuse, rogue dependency execution, SSRF.</li> <li>Mitigation: Execution Security (Plane A). Robust containerization, process sandboxing, egress limits. Traceability aids forensics but does not stop escape.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-d-persistence-attacks","title":"Threat Class D: Persistence Attacks","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Tainted memory inheritance, corrupt writes, orphaned logic (\"Ghosts\"), false consensus solidified by multi-agent echo chambers.</li> <li>Mitigation: Invariant 2 &amp; 3. Taint tracking explicitly isolates corrupt branches.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-e-governance-failure","title":"Threat Class E: Governance Failure","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Over-broad permissions leading to high-impact automation failure, role drift, irreversible autonomous damage.</li> <li>Mitigation: Risk Tiers (Section 4) bound automated actions to Tier 0-2 unconditionally, demanding specific grants for Tiers 3-4.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#6-persistence-semantics-consensus","title":"6. Persistence Semantics &amp; Consensus","text":"<ul> <li>Immutability vs State Correctness: The CORTEX Ledger is append-only and immutable. If an incorrect state is committed, the hashes prove who committed what, and when. State correction is handled via Supersession Semantics (a new fact supersedes the old) while maintaining the original forensic trail.</li> <li>Consensus does not equal Truth: If a 5-agent swarm achieves 100% consensus on a fact, the system tags the confidence high but STILL records it as probabilistic conjecture until it crosses an empirical validation test. </li> </ul> <p>CORTEX Security Spec v1 - Approved for implementation in System Gates.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/","title":"RFC: SORTU-\u03a9 Public SDK Surface v0.2","text":"<p>Status: Draft Program: ORTU-\u03a9 Forge Codename: SORTU-\u03a9 Last Updated: 2026-03-14 Audience: SDK consumers, platform engineers, compliance integrators, swarm-runtime developers</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#1-purpose","title":"1. Purpose","text":"<p>SORTU-\u03a9 provides a stateful trust layer for agent systems through typed APIs for:</p> <ul> <li>Memory \u2014 persistent storage, retrieval, temporal reconstruction</li> <li>Traceability \u2014 causal lineage and auditability</li> <li>Verification \u2014 guarded execution, integrity checks, trust degradation</li> <li>Coordination \u2014 agent registration, voting, consensus state, event emission</li> <li>Runtime \u2014 health, capability visibility, recovery truth</li> </ul> <p>This RFC defines the public SDK boundary for v1.x.</p> <p>The goal is not to expose internal architecture. The goal is to provide a clean, stable, inspectable contract that lets external consumers use the system without understanding its internal retrieval engines, cryptographic structures, or consensus internals.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#2-design-principles","title":"2. Design Principles","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#21-one-intent-one-entrypoint","title":"2.1 One intent, one entrypoint","text":"<p>Consumers express what they want, not which subsystem they want to fight.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#22-no-silent-degradation","title":"2.2 No silent degradation","text":"<p>Important fallbacks MUST become visible in: - operation results - query plans - runtime health - warnings</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#23-rejection-is-not-failure","title":"2.3 Rejection is not failure","text":"<p>The SDK MUST distinguish: - success - policy/guard rejection - system/runtime failure</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#24-runtime-is-source-of-truth","title":"2.4 Runtime is source of truth","text":"<p>Feature availability is determined by <code>capabilities()</code> and <code>health()</code>, not by docs alone.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#25-public-api-hides-internal-ontology","title":"2.5 Public API hides internal ontology","text":"<p>Terms tied to implementation details SHOULD remain internal unless they create stable, externally observable semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#26-stability-beats-cleverness","title":"2.6 Stability beats cleverness","text":"<p>The surface must be boring enough to survive production.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#3-stability-compatibility-contract","title":"3. If <code>strategy=\"auto\"</code>, <code>plan.selected_strategy</code> MUST be present. - If a meaningful fallback occurs, <code>plan.fallback_used=True</code> MUST be set and <code>fallback_reason</code> SHOULD be present. - <code>event_bus</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#57-event-envelope","title":"5.7 Event envelope","text":"<pre><code>class EventEnvelope(TypedDict, total=False):\n    schema_version: str\n    event_id: str\n    event_type: str\n    ts: str\n    tenant_id: str\n    project: str\n    source: str\n    sequence: int\n    idempotency_key: str\n    payload: Dict[str, Any]\n</code></pre> <p>Delivery semantics - Webhook delivery is at-least-once - SSE delivery is best-effort live stream - <code>idempotency_key</code> SHOULD be stable across redelivery of the same logical event - <code>sequence</code> SHOULD be monotonic per source stream where feasible</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#6-canonical-api-surface","title":"6. Canonical API Surface","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#61-memory-api","title":"6.1 Memory API","text":"<p><code>store(input: StoreInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Stores a fact, memory item, decision artifact, or related stateful object.</p> <p>Expected behavior: - passes through guard and/or membrane layers where configured - writes into storage if accepted - emits ledger/audit signals where enabled - returns warnings when operation succeeds under degraded runtime conditions</p> <p><code>query(input: QueryInput) -&gt; QueryResult</code> Primary read operation for memory retrieval.</p> <p>Expected behavior: - routes retrieval according to intent, strategy, runtime availability, and health - exposes selected plan and fallback behavior - returns evidence level at item level where possible</p> <p><code>history(input: HistoryInput) -&gt; HistoryResult</code> Returns historical evolution of a fact or entity.</p> <p><code>time_travel(input: TimeTravelInput) -&gt; TimeTravelResult</code> Returns reconstructed state or retrieval results at a specific past instant.</p> <p><code>compact(input: CompactInput) -&gt; CompactResult</code> Tier: Premium Performs memory compaction, clustering, deduplication, or consolidation under configured policy.</p> <p><code>export_working_memory(input?: WorkingMemoryExportInput) -&gt; WorkingMemoryExportResult</code> Status: Beta / Planned unless fully backed by implementation Exports session-scoped or working-memory state in a portable representation.</p> <p>Public documentation MUST NOT imply deterministic hot failover or full replay guarantees unless explicitly implemented and documented.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#62-trace-api","title":"6.2 Trace API","text":"<p><code>trace(input: TraceInput) -&gt; TraceResult</code> Returns causal lineage for a target entity.</p> <p><code>TraceInput</code> SHOULD support one or more of: - <code>tx_id</code> - <code>fact_id</code> - <code>decision_id</code> - <code>query_result_id</code> - <code>depth</code></p> <p><code>trace()</code> is for causality, not primary retrieval.</p> <p><code>audit(input: AuditInput) -&gt; AuditResult</code> Generates an operational trust/audit report.</p> <p><code>export_audit(input: ExportAuditInput) -&gt; ExportAuditResult</code> Tier: Premium Exports audit state in canonical JSON, with optional adapters to external formats.</p> <p>v1 canonical export SHOULD be stable JSON first. Additional export dialects MAY be layered later.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#63-verification-api","title":"6.3 Verification API","text":"<p><code>guard_check(input: GuardCheckInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Runs a guard/policy check without necessarily performing a write.</p> <p><code>verify(input: VerifyInput) -&gt; VerifyResult</code> Verifies integrity and trust state over a requested scope.</p> <p>Supported verification scopes SHOULD include: - <code>fact</code> - <code>project</code> - <code>tenant</code> - <code>time_range</code> - <code>tx_chain</code></p> <p><code>VerifyResult</code> SHOULD indicate: - verified scope - number of checked elements - failures found - whether legacy verification paths were involved - whether verification was partial or full</p> <p><code>taint_status(input: TaintStatusInput) -&gt; TaintStatusResult</code> Basic current taint visibility MAY exist in open tier. Persistent taint propagation and policy-driven escalation MAY be premium.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#64-coordination-api","title":"6.4 Coordination API","text":"<p><code>register_agent(input: RegisterAgentInput) -&gt; RegisterAgentResult</code> Registers an agent in the coordination layer.</p> <p><code>vote(input: VoteInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Submits a vote/opinion into the coordination system.</p> <p>Potential rejection/failure causes include: - policy block - stale agent - insufficient quorum - capability disabled - deprecated path usage - invalid scope</p> <p><code>consensus_status(input: ConsensusStatusInput) -&gt; ConsensusStatusResult</code> Returns current consensus state, status, or aggregation result for a task/topic/group.</p> <p><code>publish_event(input: PublishEventInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Emits a swarm/coordination event into the event system.</p> <p><code>publish_event</code> is preferred over vague names like <code>swarm_event</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#65-runtime-api","title":"6.5 Runtime API","text":"<p><code>health() -&gt; HealthReport</code> Returns current runtime health.</p> <p><code>capabilities() -&gt; CapabilityReport</code> Returns machine-readable capability flags and tiers.</p> <p><code>recovery_status() -&gt; RecoveryReport</code> Returns current recovery state, last checkpoint/resume report, or pending recovery warnings where implemented.</p> <p><code>memory_stats() -&gt; MemoryStats</code> Returns memory/storage operational metrics.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#7-query-routing-semantics","title":"7. Query Routing Semantics","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#71-intent","title":"7.1 Intent","text":"<p>The consumer expresses why they are querying. - <code>lookup</code> \u2014 retrieve concrete facts or state - <code>explore</code> \u2014 widen search semantically or relationally - <code>audit</code> \u2014 prioritize explainability, provenance, and trust evidence</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#72-strategy","title":"7.2 Strategy","text":"<p>The consumer expresses how strictly they want the engine to route. - <code>auto</code> \u2014 engine selects best route - <code>text</code> \u2014 text-first retrieval - <code>vector</code> \u2014 vector-first retrieval - <code>hybrid</code> \u2014 hybrid retrieval - <code>temporal</code> \u2014 temporal/historical route - <code>graph</code> \u2014 graph-enriched route</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#73-required-routing-behavior","title":"7.3 Required routing behavior","text":"<p>If strategy != \"auto\" - the engine SHOULD honor the strategy when capability and health allow - if the strategy is unavailable and fallback is not allowed by contract, return <code>FailureResult</code> or equivalent operation failure semantics - if fallback is allowed, it MUST be visible in <code>QueryPlan</code></p> <p>If strategy == \"auto\" the engine MAY select based on: - <code>intent</code> - <code>as_of</code> - <code>runtime health</code> - <code>available capabilities</code> - <code>requested evidence level</code> or <code>trace inclusion</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#74-suggested-routing-policy","title":"7.4 Suggested routing policy","text":"<p>intent=\"lookup\" 1. temporal if <code>as_of</code> is present 2. hybrid if available 3. text fallback</p> <p>intent=\"explore\" 1. graph if available and relevant 2. hybrid 3. vector 4. text fallback</p> <p>intent=\"audit\" 1. temporal and/or hybrid with trace enrichment 2. exclude or downgrade low-provenance results where possible 3. mark degraded mode aggressively if trust signal quality drops</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#8-degraded-mode-semantics","title":"8. Degraded Mode Semantics","text":"<p>The system MUST NOT silently pretend that degraded results are normal when trust or retrieval quality materially changes.</p> <p>Examples of degradation that SHOULD become visible - embedder unavailable - vector search disabled - graph enrichment unavailable - ledger verification pending or stale - taint propagation unavailable - coordination liveness uncertain - export adapter unavailable</p> <p>Visibility requirements</p> <p>Degradation SHOULD be reflected in one or more of: - <code>HealthReport.status</code> - <code>HealthReport.degraded_features</code> - <code>AcceptanceResult.warnings</code> - <code>QueryPlan.degraded_mode</code> - <code>QueryPlan.fallback_reason</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#9-error-and-rejection-code-registry","title":"9. Non-Goals for v1.x","text":"<p>The following are explicitly out of scope unless separately ratified: - guaranteed deterministic hot failover from working-memory exports - full formal proof guarantees from verification terminology alone - multi-cloud backend abstraction guarantees - external transport lock-in to Kafka or equivalent heavy brokers - exposure of raw internal graph, CRDT, or ledger implementation details as public API requirements</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#15-final-position","title":"15. Final Position","text":"<p>The public SDK must present SORTU-\u03a9 not as a pile of clever subsystems, but as a trustworthy operational contract.</p> <p>The engine may remain sophisticated internally. The surface must remain disciplined externally.</p> <p>That is the difference between infrastructure that gets admired and infrastructure that gets adopted.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/","title":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL.md \u2014 CORTEX Persist","text":"<p>Package: cortex-persist v0.3.0b2 \u00b7 Engine: v8 License: Apache-2.0 \u00b7 Python: &gt;=3.10</p> <p>This document describes trust boundaries and cognitive/state-mutation risks.</p> <p>For vulnerability disclosure policy and repository security reporting, see <code>SECURITY.md</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#purpose-the-doctrinal-formula","title":"Purpose: The Doctrinal Formula","text":"<p>CORTEX-Persist no conf\u00eda en outputs. La probabilidad puede sugerir. Solo la verificaci\u00f3n puede gobernar.</p> <p>This document defines the trust boundaries, guarantees, non-guarantees, and verification surfaces of CORTEX Persist.</p> <p>The fundamental problem of modern AI agents is not that they hallucinate; it is that their probabilistic output is granted ontological status before being verified. We do not build systems that trust the model. We build systems where unverified probabilistic output cannot survive long enough to matter. CORTEX treats all generative output as thermodynamically unstable conjecture (<code>Void-State</code>).</p> <p>CORTEX is not secure because it stores data. CORTEX is secure to the extent that it constrains who may mutate state, under which conditions, forcing probabilistic suggestions to cross a deterministic admission pipeline\u2014formal schema validation, cryptographic ledger inscription, and causal taint tracking\u2014before becoming actionable memory.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#security-posture","title":"Security Posture","text":"<p>CORTEX assumes that non-trivial inputs may be malformed, deceptive, stale, contradictory, or adversarial.</p> <p>This includes:</p> <ul> <li>human input</li> <li>agent input</li> <li>upstream model output</li> <li>tool return values</li> <li>external API responses</li> <li>replayed or duplicated facts</li> <li>semantically valid but operationally dangerous content</li> </ul> <p>Generative outputs are treated as proposals, not trusted state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#trust-boundary","title":"Trust Boundary","text":"<p>The core trust boundary is the write path.</p> <p>A proposal may only become durable state after crossing deterministic controls:</p> <pre><code>proposal\n  \u2192 guards\n  \u2192 schema/type validation\n  \u2192 policy/admission checks\n  \u2192 encryption\n  \u2192 ledger/audit recording\n  \u2192 persistence\n</code></pre> <p>If any required control fails, the write aborts.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#security-goals","title":"Security Goals","text":"<p>CORTEX aims to provide:</p> <ul> <li>durable auditability of write behavior</li> <li>cryptographic continuity across facts</li> <li>encrypted storage of sensitive fact content and metadata</li> <li>tenant-aware data isolation</li> <li>deterministic rejection of structurally invalid inputs</li> <li>explicit validation boundaries between stochastic proposals and durable state</li> <li>inspectable failure rather than silent permissiveness</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guarantees","title":"Guarantees","text":"<p>CORTEX is designed to guarantee, within correct implementation and deployment:</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#1-cryptographic-traceability","title":"1. Cryptographic Traceability","text":"<p>Persisted facts participate in a ledger continuity model through hash linking.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#2-encrypted-at-rest-storage","title":"2. Encrypted At-Rest Storage","text":"<p>Sensitive content and metadata are encrypted before persistence.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#3-auditable-write-path","title":"3. Auditable Write Path","text":"<p>State mutation is expected to emit audit-relevant trace or log data.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#4-deterministic-structural-rejection","title":"4. Deterministic Structural Rejection","text":"<p>Inputs that fail required syntax, type, schema, or guard conditions should be rejected.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#5-tenant-aware-isolation","title":"5. Tenant-Aware Isolation","text":"<p>Data operations are expected to preserve tenant boundary semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#6-failure-locality","title":"6. Failure Locality","text":"<p>Invalid proposals should fail before contaminating downstream durable state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#non-guarantees","title":"Non-Guarantees","text":"<p>CORTEX does not guarantee:</p> <ul> <li>semantic truth</li> <li>correctness of external models</li> <li>safety of upstream tools or providers</li> <li>correctness of external APIs</li> <li>absence of malicious intent in upstream sources</li> <li>correctness of business logic added outside trust boundaries</li> <li>safety if callers bypass guards, policy, or verification surfaces</li> <li>magical immunity from bad architecture layered above it</li> </ul> <p>A cryptographically logged lie is still a lie. It is merely an auditable lie.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#trust-surface-threat-model","title":"Trust-Surface Threat Model","text":"<p>Representative threats include:</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#prompt-instruction-injection","title":"Prompt / Instruction Injection","text":"<p>Inputs attempt to coerce the system into bypassing intended policy or semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#contradictory-state-insertion","title":"Contradictory State Insertion","text":"<p>Inputs attempt to create durable memory that conflicts with validated prior state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#dependency-contamination","title":"Dependency Contamination","text":"<p>Proposal depends on false, stale, or unverifiable upstream assumptions.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#silent-schema-drift","title":"Silent Schema Drift","text":"<p>A change in structure weakens invariants without obvious runtime failure.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-integrity-break","title":"Ledger Integrity Break","text":"<p>Hash continuity becomes invalid through bug, corruption, or unreviewed mutation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#replay-duplication","title":"Replay / Duplication","text":"<p>Previously accepted facts are reintroduced in a way that degrades retrieval or alters interpretation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#cross-tenant-leakage","title":"Cross-Tenant Leakage","text":"<p>Data path accidentally leaks or merges tenant state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#async-safety-failure","title":"Async Safety Failure","text":"<p>Blocking or cancellation bugs produce state inconsistency or partial operations.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#defense-in-depth","title":"Defense in Depth","text":"<p>CORTEX relies on layered control, not a single magic wall.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guards-layer","title":"Guards Layer","text":"<p>Examples include:</p> <ul> <li>contradiction guard</li> <li>dependency guard</li> <li>injection-detection and input-hardening controls</li> </ul> <p>These operate before persistence and are intended to reject invalid or dangerous proposals.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#policy-gate-layer","title":"Policy / Gate Layer","text":"<p>Admission control and policy evaluation determine whether a write is even allowed under the current actor, tenant, or environment.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#crypto-layer","title":"Crypto Layer","text":"<p>Encryption protects sensitive fact content and metadata at rest.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-layer","title":"Ledger Layer","text":"<p>Hash chaining provides continuity and tamper-evidence characteristics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#audit-layer","title":"Audit Layer","text":"<p>Audit records preserve post-hoc inspectability of critical mutations.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#verification-layer","title":"Verification Layer","text":"<p>Formal or deterministic verifiers can be used where applicable to validate invariants or reject invalid state transitions.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#verification-membrane","title":"Verification Membrane","text":"<p>The verification membrane is the boundary between generative uncertainty and durable system state.</p> <pre><code>LLM / tool proposal\n  \u2192 guard validation\n  \u2192 schema / type checks\n  \u2192 policy gate\n  \u2192 external verification where required\n  \u2192 cryptographic logging\n  \u2192 persistence\n</code></pre> <p>The membrane exists to convert unconstrained suggestion into constrained mutation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-integrity","title":"Ledger Integrity","text":"<p><code>ledger.py</code> is a critical trust surface.</p> <p>Its purpose is not branding. Its purpose is continuity.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#required-properties","title":"Required Properties","text":"<ul> <li>append-like continuity semantics</li> <li>deterministic hash derivation behavior</li> <li>stable verification path</li> <li>test coverage for continuity assumptions</li> <li>explicit review for any change affecting link formation or verification</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#practical-rule","title":"Practical Rule","text":"<p>If a change can break historical verifiability, it is not a normal refactor. It is a trust event.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#encryption-model","title":"Encryption Model","text":"<p>Fact content and meta are encrypted at rest.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#operational-meaning","title":"Operational Meaning","text":"<ul> <li>sensitive payloads must not be stored plaintext</li> <li>decryption occurs on authorized read path</li> <li>key handling must remain externalized and controlled</li> <li>secrets must not be reintroduced unencrypted into secondary stores</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#practical-rule-encryption","title":"Practical Rule (Encryption)","text":"<p>\"Encrypted primary store + plaintext side channel\" is not security. It is cosplay.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#tenant-isolation","title":"Tenant Isolation","text":"<p>All new data operations should be tenant-aware by default.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#risks","title":"Risks","text":"<ul> <li>implicit defaulting in unsafe contexts</li> <li>missing filters in read/search paths</li> <li>merged indexing or caching across tenant surfaces</li> <li>admin tooling that assumes single-tenant world state</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#expectation","title":"Expectation","text":"<p>Isolation must be explicit in APIs, search, caching, and persistence behavior.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#audit-trails","title":"Audit Trails","text":"<p>The audit system exists for:</p> <ul> <li>forensic reconstruction</li> <li>compliance evidence</li> <li>mutation traceability</li> <li>incident investigation</li> <li>operational trust</li> </ul> <p>Auditability is not a substitute for correctness. It is the condition that makes correctness failures inspectable.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#compliance-posture","title":"Compliance Posture","text":"<p>CORTEX positions audit trails, decision traceability, and deterministic validation as foundations for regulated AI environments, including EU AI Act-adjacent needs.</p> <p>This should be stated carefully.</p> <p>Correct Claim: \"The system is designed to support traceability, auditability, and constrained state mutation.\"</p> <p>Weak Claim: \"The system is compliant because we said 'compliance' near some hashes.\"</p> <p>Compliance is not a vibe. It is evidence.</p> <p>For vulnerability disclosure and reporting, see <code>SECURITY.md</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#failure-domains","title":"Failure Domains","text":"<p>Critical failure domains include:</p> <ul> <li>write-path validation</li> <li>ledger integrity</li> <li>migrations</li> <li>tenant isolation</li> <li>async transactional correctness</li> <li>policy bypass</li> <li>index consistency</li> <li>external provider misclassification or drift</li> </ul> <p>These domains deserve explicit review before release.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#release-review-checklist-for-trust-surfaces","title":"Release Review Checklist for Trust Surfaces","text":"<p>Before release, review:</p> <ul> <li>guard behavior changes</li> <li>ledger continuity changes</li> <li>encryption path changes</li> <li>policy/gate changes</li> <li>migration safety</li> <li>tenant isolation behavior</li> <li>async cancellation/timeout behavior</li> <li>audit event completeness</li> <li>API surfaces that can mutate state</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guiding-rule","title":"Guiding Rule","text":"<p>CORTEX does not make models truthful. Purpose","text":"<p>This document defines the trust semantics that govern SORTU-\u03a9 public behavior.</p> <p><code>SDK-SURFACE.md</code> defines the shape of the API. This document defines the meaning of its outputs.</p> <p>It specifies:</p> <ul> <li>evidence levels</li> <li>degraded mode semantics</li> <li>rejection vs failure semantics</li> <li>integrity semantics</li> <li>taint semantics</li> <li>trust expectations for retrieval, write, verification, and coordination flows</li> </ul> <p>The goal is simple:</p> <p>the system must not merely produce results; it must make their trust posture legible.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#2-trust-model","title":"2. Trust Model","text":"<p>SORTU-\u03a9 is not a generic retrieval engine. It is a stateful trust layer.</p> <p>Trust is represented through composition of:</p> <ol> <li>provenance \u2014 where a result came from</li> <li>causality \u2014 how it came to exist</li> <li>integrity \u2014 whether its surrounding chain is structurally valid</li> <li>policy posture \u2014 whether it passed applicable rules</li> <li>taint posture \u2014 whether error or contamination risk has propagated into it</li> <li>runtime posture \u2014 whether the system is operating in normal or degraded conditions</li> </ol> <p>Trust is never binary in the public surface. It is exposed as a set of machine-readable signals.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#3-foundational-principles","title":"3. Foundational Principles","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#31-no-false-confidence","title":"3.1 No false confidence","text":"<p>The system MUST NOT present degraded, weakly sourced, or weakly verified results as if they were equivalent to fully traceable or verified results.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#32-silence-is-not-neutrality","title":"3.2 Silence is not neutrality","text":"<p>If important retrieval, policy, or verification signals are missing, that absence SHOULD be surfaced as reduced trust, not hidden as normalcy.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#33-rejection-is-informative","title":"3.3 Rejection is informative","text":"<p>A rejected operation is still a successful act of governance if the rejection is intentional, explainable, and machine-readable.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#34-failure-is-operational-not-moral","title":"3.4 Failure is operational, not moral","text":"<p>A component outage or dependency failure is not a policy decision. It MUST be represented as a technical failure, not disguised as rejection.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#35-evidence-is-cumulative","title":"3.5 Evidence is cumulative","text":"<p>Trust increases when multiple dimensions align: - provenance exists - lineage exists - integrity holds - taint is absent or low - runtime is healthy</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#36-degradation-must-be-visible","title":"3.6 Degradation must be visible","text":"<p>If runtime degradation materially changes retrieval quality, verification power, or governance enforcement, public outputs MUST reflect it.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#4-trust-dimensions","title":"4. Trust Dimensions","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#41-provenance","title":"4.1 Provenance","text":"<p>Provenance answers: - where did this item come from? - what subsystem produced or stored it? - what identifiers anchor it?</p> <p>Minimum provenance may include: - source subsystem - fact/tx/decision identifier - timestamp - tenant/project scope</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#42-causality","title":"4.2 Causality","text":"<p>Causality answers: - what prior decision, write, or event led to this item? - what parent or predecessor relationship exists? - can lineage be traversed?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#43-integrity","title":"4.3 Integrity","text":"<p>Integrity answers: - is the relevant chain structurally intact? - does the system have evidence that ledger/hash/Merkle checks passed? - was verification full, partial, or unavailable?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#44-policy-posture","title":"4.4 Policy posture","text":"<p>Policy posture answers: - was the operation or item accepted under current rules? - was it blocked, quarantined, downgraded, or passed?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#45-taint-posture","title":"4.5 Taint posture","text":"<p>Taint posture answers: - is the item downstream from known contradictions, failures, or suspect parents? - has trust been downgraded through propagation?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#46-runtime-posture","title":"4.6 Runtime posture","text":"<p>Runtime posture answers: - was the system healthy when the operation occurred? - were required capabilities available? - were important fallbacks triggered?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#5-evidence-levels","title":"5. Evidence Levels","text":"<p>Evidence levels communicate the minimum trust visibility attached to a result.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#51-canonical-levels","title":"5.1 Canonical levels","text":"<pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass QueryEvidenceLevel(TypedDict):\n    level: Literal[\"none\", \"basic\", \"traceable\", \"verified\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#52-semantics","title":"5.2 Semantics","text":"<p>none The result exists, but the public surface cannot attach meaningful provenance or trust evidence.</p> <p>Typical cases: - raw text match - minimal metadata - transient or weakly anchored retrieval - degraded subsystem with no lineage available</p> <p>This level SHOULD be treated as informational, not strongly trusted.</p> <p>basic The result has basic provenance and metadata but no strong causal chain.</p> <p>Typical cases: - source and timestamp known - stable fact ID exists - no trace expansion requested or available - integrity state not attached</p> <p>This level is usable for general retrieval but weak for audit or compliance workflows.</p> <p>traceable The result can be linked to causal lineage.</p> <p>Typical cases: - causal chain exists - trace ID or parent decision link exists - provenance is stable enough to follow backward</p> <p>This is the minimum serious level for trust-centric workflows.</p> <p>verified The result is linked to or embedded in verified integrity state.</p> <p>Typical cases: - ledger/hash/Merkle checks passed - verification completed recently enough to be relevant - chain state is structurally valid - evidence is not merely present, but structurally checked</p> <p>This is the strongest public trust level.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#53-rules","title":"5.3 Rules","text":"<ul> <li>Systems MUST NOT emit <code>verified</code> without actual integrity basis.</li> <li>Systems SHOULD NOT emit <code>traceable</code> if no meaningful lineage can be traversed.</li> <li>Systems MAY downgrade a result\u2019s evidence level under degraded mode.</li> <li><code>intent=\"audit\"</code> SHOULD preferentially return <code>traceable</code> or <code>verified</code> items where possible.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#6-trust-grades-optional-aggregate-view","title":"6. Trust Grades (Optional Aggregate View)","text":"<p>Some consumers may want a higher-level summary beyond evidence levels.</p> <p>A runtime MAY expose an aggregate trust grade:</p> <pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass TrustGrade(TypedDict):\n    grade: Literal[\"T0\", \"T1\", \"T2\", \"T3\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre> <p>Suggested interpretation - T0 \u2014 low trust / informational only - T1 \u2014 provenance known - T2 \u2014 causally traceable - T3 \u2014 integrity-backed / verified</p> <p>This is optional and MUST NOT replace the underlying explicit signals.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#7-degraded-mode-semantics","title":"7. Degraded Mode Semantics","text":"<p>Degraded mode is a first-class trust signal, not a hidden implementation detail.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#71-definition","title":"7.1 Definition","text":"<p>A system is in degraded mode when one or more unavailable, stale, or impaired components materially affects: - retrieval quality - verification power - policy enforcement - coordination confidence - export fidelity</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#72-examples","title":"7.2 Examples","text":"<p>The following SHOULD trigger visible degradation signals when materially relevant: - embedder unavailable - vector retrieval disabled - graph enrichment unavailable - ledger verification stale or pending - taint propagation unavailable - event bus unavailable where required for coordination visibility - agent liveness unknown - export adapter unavailable - memory portability/export unavailable when explicitly requested</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#73-visibility-requirements","title":"7.3 Visibility requirements","text":"<p>Degraded mode SHOULD become visible through one or more of: - <code>HealthReport.status = \"degraded\"</code> - <code>HealthReport.degraded_features</code> - <code>AcceptanceResult.warnings</code> - <code>QueryPlan.degraded_mode = true</code> - <code>QueryPlan.fallback_reason</code> - lowered <code>QueryEvidenceLevel</code> - <code>FailureResult</code> when a forced strategy or capability cannot be honored</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#74-required-behavior-under-degraded-mode","title":"7.4 Required behavior under degraded mode","text":"<p>For strategy=\"auto\" The system MAY fallback to a weaker route, but MUST expose: - chosen strategy - fallback reason - degraded mode</p> <p>For forced strategy requests If the requested route cannot be honored: - return <code>FailureResult</code>, or - only fallback if explicit contract allows it</p> <p>Silent substitution is forbidden.</p> <p>For intent=\"audit\" The system SHOULD be more conservative: - downgrade confidence aggressively when evidence weakens - prefer fewer trustworthy results over many weak ones - surface provenance gaps - avoid presenting degraded results as equivalent to healthy-path results</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#8-rejection-vs-failure-semantics","title":"8. Rejection vs Failure Semantics","text":"<p>This distinction is non-negotiable.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#81-rejection","title":"8.1 Rejection","text":"<p>A rejection means:</p> <p>the system intentionally refused an operation according to rule, policy, trust posture, or governance logic.</p> <p>It is not a crash. It is not a missing dependency. It is an act of enforcement.</p> <p>Canonical shape:</p> <pre><code>class RejectionResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"rejection\"]\n    category: Literal[\"policy\", \"safety\", \"consistency\", \"integrity\", \"compliance\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    layer: Literal[\"guard\", \"membrane\", \"policy\", \"verification\"]\n    rule_id: str\n    severity: Literal[\"low\", \"medium\", \"high\", \"critical\"]\n    evidence: list\n    remediation: list[str]\n    metadata: dict\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#82-failure","title":"8.2 Failure","text":"<p>A failure means:</p> <p>the system could not perform the requested operation due to runtime or dependency conditions.</p> <p>Canonical shape:</p> <pre><code>class FailureResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"failure\"]\n    category: Literal[\"dependency\", \"storage\", \"runtime\", \"timeout\", \"capability\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    retryable: bool\n    component: str\n    metadata: dict\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#83-hard-rules","title":"8.3 Hard rules","text":"<ul> <li>Dependency outages MUST NOT be represented as rejections.</li> <li>Policy blocks MUST NOT be represented as failures.</li> <li>Consumers MUST be able to distinguish governance posture from runtime instability.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#84-examples","title":"8.4 Examples","text":"<p>Rejection - contradiction detected - taint threshold exceeded - policy blocked scope - deprecated write path blocked - quorum denied under governance rules</p> <p>Failure - embedder offline - database lock timeout - ledger I/O failure - capability disabled at runtime - event dispatch failure - verification scope invalid due to malformed input</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#9-integrity-semantics","title":"9. Integrity Semantics","text":"<p>Integrity is the public representation of structural validity around stored or inferred state.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#91-integrity-does-not-mean-truth","title":"9.1 Integrity does not mean truth","text":"<p>Integrity means: - chain structure is consistent - cryptographic or structural checks passed - the record has not obviously drifted from its expected lineage or checkpoint basis</p> <p>It does NOT mean: - the content is philosophically true - the source is omniscient - the model never hallucinated upstream</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#92-public-integrity-states","title":"9.2 Public integrity states","text":"<pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass IntegrityState(TypedDict):\n    status: Literal[\"unknown\", \"partial\", \"verified\", \"failed\", \"stale\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#93-semantics","title":"9.3 Semantics","text":"<ul> <li>unknown \u2014 no integrity evaluation available</li> <li>partial \u2014 only subset or limited path checked</li> <li>verified \u2014 requested or linked integrity checks passed</li> <li>failed \u2014 structural check failed</li> <li>stale \u2014 integrity state exists but is too old or insufficiently current for strong claims</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#94-verification-scopes","title":"9.4 Verification scopes","text":"<p>Public verification SHOULD distinguish scope: - fact - project - tenant - time range - tx chain</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#95-verify-result-expectations","title":"9.5 Verify result expectations","text":"<p>A <code>VerifyResult</code> SHOULD report: - requested scope - checked item count - failed item count - integrity state - legacy path involvement - full vs partial verification</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#96-legacy-path-warning","title":"9.6 Legacy path warning","text":"<p>If integrity verification depends on legacy hash or schema paths, that fact SHOULD be surfaced in: - warnings - metadata - verify result notes</p> <p>Because \u201cverified\u201d by archaeological compatibility is not the same as \u201cverified\u201d on clean modern rails.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#10-taint-semantics","title":"10. Taint Semantics","text":"<p>Taint models downstream trust contamination or risk inheritance.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#101-definition","title":"10.1 Definition","text":"<p>Taint is a trust degradation signal that propagates through dependency or causality structures when upstream inputs, decisions, or facts are suspect, contradictory, compromised, or insufficiently trustworthy.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#102-taint-is-not-binary","title":"10.2 Taint is not binary","text":"<p>Public surfaces SHOULD expose at least a small number of taint states.</p> <pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass TaintState(TypedDict):\n    status: Literal[\"none\", \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\", \"unknown\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#103-suggested-interpretation","title":"10.3 Suggested interpretation","text":"<ul> <li>none \u2014 no active taint detected</li> <li>low \u2014 weak taint signals or low-confidence contamination path</li> <li>medium \u2014 meaningful contamination risk</li> <li>high \u2014 serious contamination risk; downstream trust strongly reduced</li> <li>unknown \u2014 taint system unavailable or insufficiently evaluated</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#104-public-behavior","title":"10.4 Public behavior","text":"<ul> <li>high taint SHOULD strongly affect confidence, evidence level, or rejection posture</li> <li>unknown taint MUST NOT be silently treated as none</li> <li><code>intent=\"audit\"</code> SHOULD surface taint explicitly when requested or relevant</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#105-open-vs-premium-guidance","title":"10.5 Open vs premium guidance","text":"<ul> <li>Open tier MAY expose current taint visibility</li> <li>Premium tier MAY expose persistent taint propagation, policy thresholds, and exportable taint lineage</li> </ul> <p>This split is acceptable only if open tier still exposes enough truth not to mislead consumers.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#11-query-trust-semantics","title":"11. Query Trust Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#111-query-is-not-just-retrieval","title":"11.1 Query is not just retrieval","text":"<p>A query result is a trust-bearing object containing: - content - confidence - provenance posture - routing plan - degradation context - optional taint/trace posture</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#112-required-semantics-for-queryresult","title":"11.2 Required semantics for QueryResult","text":"<p>If strategy=\"auto\": - <code>plan.selected_strategy</code> MUST be present</p> <p>If fallback materially changes route: - <code>plan.fallback_used = true</code> - <code>plan.fallback_reason</code> SHOULD be present</p> <p>If runtime degradation materially affects the result: - <code>plan.degraded_mode = true</code></p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#113-intent-specific-expectations","title":"11.3 Intent-specific expectations","text":"<p>lookup Optimize for concrete retrieval with reasonable trust visibility.</p> <p>explore Optimize for breadth, relation, and expansion, but still expose degradation and provenance limits.</p> <p>audit Optimize for trust clarity: - prefer fewer, stronger results - expose evidence level clearly - expose provenance gaps - degrade conservatively - avoid overconfident ranking of weakly sourced items</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#114-confidence-vs-trust","title":"11.4 Confidence vs trust","text":"<p>Confidence score is not equivalent to trust.</p> <p>A result may have: - high lexical or vector score - low evidence level - unknown integrity - medium taint - degraded routing path</p> <p>Consumers MUST be able to distinguish \u201cmatched strongly\u201d from \u201ctrusted strongly.\u201d</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#12-write-path-trust-semantics","title":"12. Write Path Trust Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#121-a-successful-write-is-not-just-storage","title":"12.1 A successful write is not just storage","text":"<p>A successful write means the system: - accepted the operation under current trust policy - completed storage path - returned any required warnings - attached operation metadata where applicable</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#122-a-rejected-write-is-governance-not-breakage","title":"12.2 A rejected write is governance, not breakage","text":"<p>Examples: - contradiction with canonical fact - taint threshold exceeded - compliance scope violation - policy forbids raw write into protected tenant - deprecated path blocked</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#123-a-write-under-degraded-mode","title":"12.3 A write under degraded mode","text":"<p>If the write succeeds under degraded conditions, the system SHOULD warn when the degradation is materially relevant.</p> <p>Examples: - accepted without vector enrichment - accepted while compliance export unavailable - accepted while downstream event dispatch degraded</p> <p>Do not pretend the path was pristine if it was limping.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#13-coordination-trust-semantics","title":"13. Coordination Trust Semantics","text":"<p>Coordination is the noisiest place to hallucinate certainty. Do not.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#131-consensus-is-not-omniscience","title":"13.1 Consensus is not omniscience","text":"<p>Public consensus results should reflect: - participating agents - available liveness posture - confidence/weighting basis where exposed - degraded conditions - quorum state</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#132-required-caution-zones","title":"13.2 Required caution zones","text":"<p>The following conditions SHOULD reduce coordination trust or trigger warnings: - stale agents - liveness unknown - degraded event visibility - mixed legacy vote paths - insufficient quorum - disabled consensus capability - incomplete tracking path</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#133-coordination-truth-posture","title":"13.3 Coordination truth posture","text":"<p>Do not present a coordination result as fully trustworthy when: - liveness is missing - tracking is partial - vote schema is in deprecation transition - event bus evidence is missing where expected</p> <p>A swarm with no pulse is just a rumor.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#14-runtime-truth-semantics","title":"14. Runtime Truth Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#141-health-is-operational-truth-not-decoration","title":"14.1 health() is operational truth, not decoration","text":"<p><code>health()</code> must be actionable.</p> <p>Consumers should be able to use it to: - decide whether to force a strategy - detect degraded mode - gate compliance-sensitive operations - switch UI states - annotate logs or metrics</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#142-capabilities-is-contractual-truth","title":"14.2 capabilities() is contractual truth","text":"<p><code>capabilities()</code> defines what the runtime can actually do now.</p> <p>Documentation, licensing text, and roadmap prose do not override runtime flags.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#143-contractual-expectations","title":"14.3 Contractual expectations","text":"<p>If a capability is disabled: - forced use should fail clearly - auto mode may fallback if allowed - the result must not pretend premium behavior occurred</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#15-trust-aware-error-code-semantics","title":"15. Trust-Aware Error Code Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#151-rejection-codes-imply-governance-meaning","title":"15.1 Rejection codes imply governance meaning","text":"<p>Examples: - <code>ERR_CONTRADICTION</code> - <code>ERR_TAINT_HIGH</code> - <code>ERR_POLICY_BLOCK</code> - <code>ERR_COMPLIANCE_SCOPE</code> - <code>ERR_DEPRECATED_WRITE_PATH</code></p> <p>These should tell the integrator: - why it was blocked - where the block happened - what to do next</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#152-failure-codes-imply-operational-meaning","title":"15.2 Failure codes imply operational meaning","text":"<p>Examples: - <code>ERR_EMBEDDER_UNAVAILABLE</code> - <code>ERR_DB_LOCK_TIMEOUT</code> - <code>ERR_LEDGER_IO</code> - <code>ERR_EVENT_DISPATCH_FAILED</code> - <code>ERR_COMPONENT_UNAVAILABLE</code></p> <p>These should tell the integrator: - what broke - whether retry makes sense - whether degradation/fallback is possible</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#153-code-stability","title":"15.3 Code stability","text":"<p>Codes are part of the contract. Non-Goals","text":"<p>This document does not claim: - philosophical truth of content - total elimination of model error - formal proof guarantees unless explicitly implemented - perfect liveness in coordination - omniscient provenance in all retrieval paths - deterministic replay from partial working-memory exports</p> <p>This is a trust contract, not a messiah contract.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#18-final-position","title":"18. Final Position","text":"<p>The public system must make one thing unmistakably clear:</p> <p>a result is not just returned; it is situated.</p> <p>Its route, evidence, degradation, integrity posture, taint posture, and governance status must be legible enough that an external consumer can decide what to do next without guessing.</p> <p>That is the threshold between \u201cclever memory system\u201d and \u201cinfrastructure that can be trusted under pressure.\u201d</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/","title":"CORTEX v6 Architecture: The Sovereign Cloud","text":"<p>Status: Historical v6 architecture snapshot. Refer to current architecture docs for live behavior.</p> <p>Level 130/100 Enterprise Sovereign Platform</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#1-multi-tenant-isolation-hierarchy","title":"1. Multi-Tenant Isolation Hierarchy","text":"<p>v6 introduces strict cryptographic and logical isolation. Data belonging to a <code>tenant_id</code> is isolated at three levels:</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l3-isolation-ledger","title":"L3 Isolation (Ledger)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: PostgreSQL / AlloyDB with Row-Level Security (RLS).</li> <li>Enforcement: Each database session is scoped to a specific <code>tenant_id</code>.</li> <li>Query: <code>SELECT * FROM facts WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('cortex.tenant_id')</code></li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l2-isolation-vector-search","title":"L2 Isolation (Vector Search)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: Qdrant Cloud / Distributed Clusters.</li> <li>Enforcement: Payload filtering in every semantic search.</li> <li>Constraint: <code>FieldCondition(key=\"tenant_id\", match=MatchValue(value=tenant_id))</code> is automatically injected by <code>VectorStoreL2.recall()</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l1-isolation-working-memory","title":"L1 Isolation (Working Memory)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: Redis / Local LRU.</li> <li>Enforcement: Cache keys prefixed with <code>tenant_id:session_id</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#2-distributed-rbac-engine","title":"2. Distributed RBAC Engine","text":"<p>CORTEX v6 moves beyond simple API keys to a granular permission system.</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#role-definitions","title":"Role Definitions","text":"Role Scope Permissions <code>SOVEREIGN_ROOT</code> Global Can manage all tenants, billing, and infra. <code>TENANT_ADMIN</code> Tenant Can manage keys, projects, and users within a tenant. <code>AGENT_EXECUTOR</code> Project Can <code>read</code>, <code>write</code>, and <code>vote</code> on facts within specific projects. <code>AUDITOR</code> Tenant/Project Read-only access to transaction logs and Merkle checkpoints."},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#permission-scopes","title":"Permission Scopes","text":"<ul> <li><code>memory:read</code>: Access to semantic recall.</li> <li><code>memory:write</code>: Access to <code>process_interaction</code>.</li> <li><code>ledger:verify</code>: Access to integrity checks.</li> <li><code>consensus:vote</code>: Ability to influence <code>consensus_score</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#3-data-flow-distributed","title":"3. Data Flow (Distributed)","text":"<p>```mermaid sequenceDiagram     participant App as External App     participant GW as CORTEX Gateway (GraphQL)     participant RBAC as RBAC Engine     participant Cache as Redis (L1)     participant DB as AlloyDB (L3)     participant Vec as Qdrant (L2)</p> <pre><code>App-&gt;&gt;GW: Query context (JWT + Query)\nGW-&gt;&gt;RBAC: Validate Session (tenant_id, scope)\nRBAC--&gt;&gt;GW: OK (tenant-A)\nGW-&gt;&gt;Cache: Fetch recent window (tenant-A:session-X)\nCache--&gt;&gt;GW: L1 Context\nGW-&gt;&gt;Vec: Semantic search (tenant_id=\"tenant-A\")\nVec--&gt;&gt;GW: L2 Context (Isolated)\nGW-&gt;&gt;DB: Audit Log\nGW--&gt;&gt;App: Unified Context\n</code></pre> <p>```</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#4-migration-strategy-v5-v6","title":"4. Migration Strategy (v5 \u2192 v6)","text":"<ol> <li>Schema Upgrade: Run <code>cortex migrate v6</code> to add <code>tenant_id</code> columns to all tables.</li> <li>Backfill: Assign <code>default</code> tenant to all legacy records.</li> <li>Ledger Re-Hashing: Recompute Merkle trees with multi-tenant signatures.</li> <li>Porting: Swap <code>SQLiteStorage</code> for <code>PostgreSQLStorage</code> in <code>config.yaml</code>.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/","title":"CORTEX v6: Sovereign Cloud Transition Guide","text":"<p>Status: Historical transition guide. The Vision: From Daemon to Platform","text":"<p>CORTEX v5 established the foundation for a truly sovereign local AI agent system. It proved that complex cognitive memory, multi-model routing, and autonomous goal-seeking can run entirely on-device.</p> <p>v6 is the scale-up. We are transitioning from a single-user local daemon to an enterprise-grade, multi-tenant \"Sovereign Cloud\" platform.</p>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#key-objectives","title":"Key Objectives","text":"<ul> <li>Multi-Tenancy: Secure data isolation at every layer (L1, L2, L3).</li> <li>Enterprise Security: Full Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Audit Logging.</li> <li>Global Availability: Migration from local SQLite/embedded stores to distributed databases (AlloyDB, PostgreSQL) and high-availability vector clusters (Remote Qdrant).</li> <li>Infinite Memory: Scaling semantic recall across trillions of tokens without latency degradation.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#2-technical-roadmap","title":"2. Technical Roadmap","text":""},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#phase-1-foundation-current","title":"Phase 1: Foundation (Current)","text":"<ul> <li>[x] Injection of <code>tenant_id</code> into <code>CortexMemoryManager</code>.</li> <li>[/] Implementation of RBAC engine prototypes.</li> <li>[ ] Migration of the <code>EventLedgerL3</code> to support PostgreSQL as a backend option.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#phase-2-orchestration","title":"Phase 2: Orchestration","text":"<ul> <li>[ ] GraphQL API for cross-language integration (replacing raw JSON-RPC/Local sockets).</li> <li>[ ] Distributed Event Bus (NATS or Redis Stream) for swarm orchestration.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#phase-3-sovereign-cloud","title":"Phase 3: Sovereign Cloud","text":"<ul> <li>[ ] Deployment blueprints for GCP (AlloyDB + GKE + Qdrant Cloud).</li> <li>[ ] Zero-Knowledge encryption for user memories at rest.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#3-for-developers-what-changes","title":"3. It is mathematically impossible (by policy) for Tenant A to retrieve vectors belonging to Tenant B.</p>"},{"location":"V6_TRANSITION_GUIDE/#4-why-sovereign","title":"4. Why \"Sovereign\"?","text":"<p>Even as we move to the cloud, the core mission remains: User-owned Intelligence. v6 will support hybrid-cloud deployments where compute happens in the cloud but the \"Master Keys\" stay on the user's hardware.</p>"},{"location":"api/","title":"REST API Reference","text":"<p>CORTEX exposes a FastAPI application via <code>cortex.api:app</code>. Facts are immutable records with temporal validity:</p> Field Type Description <code>id</code> INTEGER Auto-incremented primary key <code>project</code> TEXT Namespace (tenant isolation) <code>content</code> TEXT The information itself <code>fact_type</code> TEXT <code>knowledge</code>, <code>decision</code>, <code>error</code>, <code>ghost</code>, <code>config</code>, <code>bridge</code>, <code>axiom</code>, <code>rule</code> <code>tags</code> JSON Searchable labels <code>confidence</code> TEXT <code>stated</code>, <code>inferred</code>, <code>observed</code>, <code>verified</code>, <code>disputed</code> <code>valid_from</code> DATETIME When the fact became true <code>valid_until</code> DATETIME When deprecated (NULL = active) <code>source</code> TEXT Origin agent or process (auto-detected) <code>meta</code> JSON Arbitrary metadata <code>consensus_score</code> REAL Weighted agreement (default 1.0) <code>tx_id</code> INTEGER FK to creating transaction <code>tenant_id</code> TEXT Multi-tenant scope"},{"location":"architecture/#temporal-queries","title":"Temporal Queries","text":"<p>Every fact has a temporal window (<code>valid_from</code> \u2192 <code>valid_until</code>):</p> <ul> <li>Current view: <code>WHERE valid_until IS NULL</code></li> <li>Point-in-time: <code>WHERE valid_from &lt;= ? AND (valid_until IS NULL OR valid_until &gt; ?)</code></li> <li>Time travel: Reconstruct database state at any transaction ID</li> <li>History: Full timeline including deprecated facts</li> </ul>"},{"location":"architecture/#hash-chained-ledger","title":"Hash-Chained Ledger","text":"<p>Every mutation creates a transaction with a SHA-256 hash linked to the previous one:</p> <pre><code>TX #1: hash = SHA256(\"GENESIS\" + project + action + detail + timestamp)\nTX #2: hash = SHA256(hash_1 + project + action + detail + timestamp)\nTX #N: hash = SHA256(hash_{N-1} + ...)\n</code></pre> <p>This creates a tamper-evident audit trail. <code>verify_ledger()</code> walks the chain and reports any breaks.</p>"},{"location":"architecture/#merkle-tree-checkpoints","title":"Merkle Tree Checkpoints","text":"<p>Periodically, the ledger creates Merkle tree checkpoints from batches of fact hashes.
