The Data Contract: <code>MacSnapshot</code>","text":"<p>The raw telemetry is strictly separated from inferred findings to prevent conflating observed state with diagnostic opinion.</p> <pre><code>@dataclass\nclass MacSnapshot:\n    platform: str          # \"darwin\" or \"unsupported\"\n    cpu_percent: float     # 0.0-100.0\n    memory_pressure: str   # \"ok\" | \"warn\" | \"critical\"\n    thermal_state: str     # \"Nominal\" | \"Fair\" | \"Serious\" | \"Critical\"\n    process_count: int     # Running process entropy\n    ax_trusted: bool       # Accessibility bridge integrity\n    gpu_active: bool       # Metal / discrete GPU load\n    timestamp: str         # ISO8601 UTC observation window\n</code></pre> <p>Guards &amp; Degradation: * If <code>sys.platform != \"darwin\"</code>, the module degrades gracefully to <code>platform=\"unsupported\"</code>, producing a neutral score (<code>100</code>) to prevent penalizing non-Mac engineers. * All parsing logic includes robust <code>try/except</code> fallbacks heavily preferring <code>Nominal</code> or <code>0.0</code> over runtime crashes.</p>"},{"location":"MEJORALO-MAC-TELEMETRY-RFC/#3-signal-extraction-methodology-zero-dependency","title":"3. Signal Extraction Methodology (Zero-Dependency)","text":"<p>The <code>mac_control</code> module adheres to the mac-maestro-omega architecture: extracting rich state using only pre-installed Unix/macOS binaries. No <code>psutil</code> or external C-extensions are permitted.</p> Metric Source Binary Heuristic CPU % <code>top -l 1</code> Parses <code>CPU usage:</code> extracting <code>idle</code> and computing <code>100 - idle</code>. Memory Pressure <code>sysctl</code>, <code>vm_stat</code> Reads <code>vm.memory_pressure</code>. Fallback: extrapolates from <code>pageouts</code>. Thermal State <code>ioreg</code>, <code>pmset</code> Scans <code>IOPlatformExpertDevice</code> for throttling status. Process Entropy <code>ps -A</code> Counts total active PIDs to measure daemon sprawl. AX Trust <code>osascript</code> Attempts to query <code>System Events</code>. Rejection implies broken automation bridge. GPU Active <code>ioreg</code> Checks <code>IOPCIDevice</code> for discrete AMD/Apple hardware engagement."},{"location":"MEJORALO-MAC-TELEMETRY-RFC/#4-taxonomy-of-findings","title":"4. Taxonomy of Findings","text":"<p>Telemetry findings are categorically distinct from standard AST or dependency findings. They are classified into a specific local-friction taxonomy:</p> Category Description Example Finding Penalty Host_Runtime CPU/Memory saturation Memory pressure CRITICAL -20 to -40 OS_Control_Plane OS-level permissions AX trust not granted -25 Thermal_Physics Hardware throttling Serious thermal state -10 to -40 Local_Entropy Daemon/Process sprawl Process entropy high (300) -5 per 10 over limit"},{"location":"MEJORALO-MAC-TELEMETRY-RFC/#5-causal-matrix-telemetry-vs-diagnostics","title":"5. Causal Matrix: Telemetry vs. Diagnostics","text":"<p>How <code>mac_control</code> signals directly alter the interpretation of standard MEJORAlo findings (Future cross-causal rules).</p> macOS Telemetry Signal Causal Impact on Code Diagnostics Recommended System Action High Memory Pressure Degrades confidence in \"slow test suite\" or \"memory leak\" code findings. The host is swapping heavily; the code may be fine. Mask performance-related warnings. Advise user to restart Docker/IDE. AX Trust Missing Explains why e2e UI tests, AppleScript, or Phantom interaction pipelines are failing. Flag as <code>CRITICAL</code> blocker. Inject explicit instructions to open System Settings. Thermal Throttling (Serious) Invalidates local benchmarking. Any <code>time.sleep()</code> or async latency measurements are currently compromised by the OS scheduler. Mark timing-based test results as <code>UNRELIABLE</code>. Process Entropy &gt; 250 Indicates systemic environmental decay (zombie node processes, orphaned workers). Suggest a <code>killall</code> cleanup before evaluating background daemons in the stack. Sustained CPU &gt; 90% Systemic saturation. Prevents accurate assessment of the project's baseline computational footprint. Pause heavy static analysis (e.g., AST parsing deep trees) until thermal/CPU returns to Nominal."},{"location":"MEJORALO-MAC-TELEMETRY-RFC/#6-evolution-path","title":"6. Evolution Path","text":"<p>v1 (Current): Contextual telemetry injected as an isolated scoring dimension. Findings are informative. v2: Cross-causal masking. If memory pressure is critical, auto-downgrade performance-related codebase findings so the developer isn't chasing phantom bottlenecks. v3: Temporal normalization. Instead of evaluating a single snapshot, <code>mac_control</code> will monitor a rolling 3-minute window to filter out transient spikes from sustained structural degradation. </p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/","title":"RFC: SORTU-\u03a9 Onboarding Flows v0.1","text":"<p>Status: Draft Program: ORTU-\u03a9 Forge Codename: SORTU-\u03a9 Last Updated: 2026-03-14 Audience: New Integrators, Swarm Developers, Platform Architects</p> <p>Classification: Active RFC for SDK onboarding flows.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#1-purpose","title":"1. Purpose","text":"<p>This document provides definitive Onboarding Flows for engineers adopting the SORTU-\u03a9 public SDK. </p> <p>Instead of reading raw endpoint documentation in isolation, integrators should start here to understand the four primary operational loops:</p> <ol> <li>Working Memory Integration (The RAG / Persistence Loop)</li> <li>Audit &amp; Trace Integration (The Compliance Loop)</li> <li>Verification Integration (The Integrity Loop)</li> <li>Coordination Integration (The Swarm Loop)</li> </ol> <p>For each flow, we define the happy path, the degraded path, and the rejection path.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#2-working-memory-integration-the-rag-loop","title":"2. Working Memory Integration (The RAG Loop)","text":"<p>This is the most common integration: storing facts and retrieving them for generation.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#21-the-write-path","title":"2.1 The Write Path","text":"<p>Goal: Persist an observation or decision.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.memory.store(fact)</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 runs policy guards, contradiction checks, and taint evaluation.</li> <li>Happy Path: Returns <code>AcceptanceResult</code>. The fact is durably stored and indexed.</li> <li>Rejection Path: Returns <code>RejectionResult</code> (e.g., <code>ERR_CONTRADICTION</code>). The client MUST NOT retry; it must resolve the semantic conflict (e.g., submit with supersession metadata).</li> <li>Failure Path: Returns <code>FailureResult</code> (e.g., <code>ERR_EMBEDDER_UNAVAILABLE</code>). The client MAY retry if the error is marked <code>retryable</code>.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#22-the-retrieval-path","title":"2.2 The Retrieval Path","text":"<p>Goal: Retrieve facts to build context for an LLM.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.memory.query(intent=\"lookup\", strategy=\"auto\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 determines the optimal execution plan.</li> <li>Happy Path: Returns <code>QueryResult</code> with vector-enriched, temporally accurate items. <code>plan.fallback_used</code> is <code>false</code>.</li> <li>Degraded Path: Returns <code>QueryResult</code> but <code>plan.degraded_mode</code> is <code>true</code> (e.g., vector search failed, fell back to basic text retrieval). </li> <li>Integration Rule: If <code>degraded_mode</code> is true, the client SHOULD warn the user or lower the temperature of the subsequent LLM generation, as the context density is compromised.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#3-audit-trace-integration-the-compliance-loop","title":"3. Audit &amp; Trace Integration (The Compliance Loop)","text":"<p>This flow is used by compliance officers, red teams, or debugging agents to understand why the system believes something.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#31-the-provenance-path","title":"3.1 The Provenance Path","text":"<p>Goal: Trace a fact back to its origin.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.trace.trace(fact_id=\"fact_123\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 traverses the causal DAG backward.</li> <li>Happy Path: Returns a <code>TraceResult</code> graph from the target fact down to the root axioms or external sensor inputs.</li> <li>Rejection Path: Returns <code>RejectionResult</code> (e.g., <code>ERR_COMPLIANCE_SCOPE</code> if the trace crosses into a tenant boundary the client cannot read).</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#32-the-trust-audit-path","title":"3.2 The Trust Audit Path","text":"<p>Goal: Prove the system's operational integrity at a specific point in time.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.trace.audit(intent=\"audit\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 generates a trust report.</li> <li>Integration Rule: The client SHOULD inspect the <code>evidence_levels</code> in the returned report. If the majority of facts are listed as <code>none</code> or <code>basic</code>, the audit fails the \"High Trust\" internal bar. The client requires <code>traceable</code> or <code>verified</code> evidence.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#4-verification-integration-the-integrity-loop","title":"4. Verification Integration (The Integrity Loop)","text":"<p>This flow is used to ensure the memory hasn't been tampered with or silently corrupted.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#41-the-snapshot-check","title":"4.1 The Snapshot Check","text":"<p>Goal: Validate that a specific project's state is cryptographically sound.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.verification.verify(scope=\"project\", project_id=\"alpha\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 recalculates the Merkle roots and cross-references the Master Ledger.</li> <li>Happy Path: Returns <code>VerifyResult</code> with status <code>verified</code>. The client can proceed with high-stakes autonomous execution.</li> <li>Failed Path: Returns status <code>failed</code>. </li> <li>Integration Rule: If <code>failed</code>, the client MUST immediately halt autonomous execution for that project and alert a human operator or the Immune System daemon.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#42-the-taint-check","title":"4.2 The Taint Check","text":"<p>Goal: Ensure a workflow isn't using contaminated data.</p> <ol> <li>Client calls <code>sdk.verification.taint_status(fact_id=\"fact_123\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 calculates the propagation of downstream risk.</li> <li>Integration Rule: If status is <code>high</code> or <code>medium</code>, the client SHOULD refuse to use the fact in a critical path (e.g., financial transactions, physical actuation) without explicit human override.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#5-coordination-integration-the-swarm-loop","title":"5. Coordination Integration (The Swarm Loop)","text":"<p>This flow is used by multi-agent swarms to vote, reach consensus, and share state.</p>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#51-the-registration-path","title":"5.1 The Registration Path","text":"<p>Goal: An agent joins the swarm and announces its capabilities.</p> <ol> <li>Client (Agent A) calls <code>sdk.coordination.register_agent()</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 active agents broadcast a <code>agent.registered</code> event.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#52-the-consensus-path","title":"5.2 The Consensus Path","text":"<p>Goal: Multiple agents vote on a decision (e.g., \"Is this PR safe to deploy?\").</p> <ol> <li>Clients (Agents A, B, C) call <code>sdk.coordination.vote(topic_id=\"deploy_42\", decision=\"approve\")</code>.</li> <li>SORTU-\u03a9 aggregates votes against quorum rules.</li> <li>Happy Path: Quorum reached. The system emits a <code>consensus.reached</code> event via Webhooks/SSE.</li> <li>Rejection Path: Returns <code>ERR_AGENT_STALE</code> for Agent C. </li> <li>Integration Rule: The swarm orchestration layer MUST listen for <code>consensus.failed</code> or <code>consensus.reached</code> events via the Event Bus, rather than polling the database.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"ONBOARDING-FLOWS/#6-real-world-anti-patterns-to-avoid","title":"6. Real-World Anti-Patterns to Avoid","text":"<ul> <li>Ignoring <code>plan.degraded_mode</code> in QueryResults. (Result: Infinite retry loops hammering the database because a fact contradicted a policy).</li> <li>Relying on Polling for Consensus. (Result: Deadlocks and timeouts. Maxwell's Demon (Admission Guard): Fact insertion requires an explicit <code>extract -&gt; consolidate -&gt; store</code> pipeline. No fact is persisted without passing the exergy threshold. 3. Ghost Annihilation: Action-observation traces must be compressed and deleted after the goal is achieved to prevent carrying semantic radiation into new cycles.</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-MEMORY-OS/#3-topologia-de-subsistemas","title":"3. Topolog\u00eda de Subsistemas","text":"<p>The Memory OS is composed of four heavily isolated modules:</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-MEMORY-OS/#31-cortexcompactionmem0_pipelinepy-the-thermodynamic-filter","title":"3.1. <code>cortex/compaction/mem0_pipeline.py</code> (The Thermodynamic Filter)","text":"<p>Responsible for preventing unverified, low-exergy conversational data from entering the CORTEX Ledger.  - Extract: Parses entities, intent, and relationships from the episodic context. - Consolidate: Resolves collisions, contradictions, and redundancy (compresses <code>H(X)</code>). - Store: Commits to the <code>ledger.py</code> explicitly as semantic/persistent memory. - Impact: Expected 90% reduction in token retrieval overhead (Mem0 baseline).</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-MEMORY-OS/#32-cortexpolicymemory_ospy-the-os-hypervisor","title":"3.2. <code>cortex/policy/memory_os.py</code> (The OS Hypervisor)","text":"<p>Enforces access and mutation policies across distinct memory domains. - Clases: <code>[Working]</code>, <code>[Episodic]</code>, <code>[Semantic]</code> - Agents do not run a \"global search\". Abstract &amp; Axiomatic Foundation","text":"<p>The current paradigm of AI agent memory relies on fundamentally flawed, probabilistic storage (Vector DBs) without cryptographic verification. This constitutes a critical vulnerability: memory without mathematical provenance is structurally indistinguishable from hallucination.</p> <p>This document formally defines the CORTEX Trust Verification Standard, a protocol designed to elevate AI agent memory from probabilistic storage to a deterministic, tamper-proof cryptographic ledger. Any system claiming CORTEX compliance MUST implement the constraints, schemas, and pipelines outlined herein.</p> <p>Foundational Axiom (\u03a9\u2083): Byzantine Default. The system must operate under the assumption that agents will hallucinate, sub-agents will fail, and input may be adversarial. Verification precedes trust.</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#2-the-cryptographic-trust-pipeline","title":"2. The Cryptographic Trust Pipeline","text":"<p>To achieve compliance, an agent memory architecture MUST process all incoming state changes (new memories, updates, deletions) through a strict, unidirectional pipeline before persistence:</p> <ol> <li>Input Capture: Raw semantic data is intercepted along with necessary environment metadata (timestamp, model ID, agent intent).</li> <li>Epistemic Guard (Validation): The input is processed through validation guards to ensure semantic non-contradiction with the existing known state.</li> <li>AES-256 Envelope Encryption: The semantic content and associated metadata are encrypted at rest.</li> <li>SHA-256 Ledger Hashing: The encrypted payload, combined with the hash of the previous verified fact, generates a new cryptographic signature, extending the immutable Ledger Chain.</li> <li>Vector Embedding (Secondary): Only after cryptographic validation is the payload embedded for semantic retrieval.</li> <li>Storage Engine: The payload, hash, and vector are committed to the persistence layer.</li> </ol> <p>Failure at any point in this pipeline MUST result in a synchronous <code>TrustViolationException</code>. Silent failures or fallback mechanisms are STRICTLY PROHIBITED (See Anti-Pattern AP-1).</p>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#3-the-sovereign-data-schema-the-fact","title":"3. The Sovereign Data Schema (The \"Fact\")","text":"<p>A compliant memory entry is defined as a <code>Fact</code>. It represents an immutable unit of truth within the system's timeline.</p> <pre><code>required_fields:\n  id: \"Sequential integer or UUIDv7 (for temporal locality)\"\n  tenant_id: \"String. Enforces strict multi-tenant hardware segregation.\"\n  project: \"String. Namespace for the target context.\"\n  fact_type: \"Enum: [decision, error, ghost, bridge, discovery, axiom]\"\n  content: \"AES-Encrypted byte array. The semantic truth.\"\n  tags: \"Array of Strings.\"\n  confidence: \"Enum: [C1, C2, C3, C4, C5]. Epistemic certainty.\"\n  valid_from: \"ISO-8601 Timestamp. When the fact became active.\"\n  source: \"String. Origin identifier (e.g., agent:gemini_3.1, user:admin).\"\n  consensus_score: \"Float [0.0 - 1.0]. Byzantine consensus weight.\"\n  hash: \"String. SHA-256 cryptographic link to prior Fact.\"\n\nconditional_fields:\n  valid_until: \"Optional ISO-8601 Timestamp. Marks archival/deprecation.\"\n  tx_id: \"Optional Integer. Batched write grouping.\"\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#4-regulatory-alignment-eu-ai-act-article-12","title":"4. Regulatory Alignment: EU AI Act (Article 12)","text":"<p>This protocol is explicitly constructed to function as a turnkey compliance engine for the EU AI Act, Article 12 (Record-Keeping) for High-Risk AI Systems.</p> AI Act Requirement (Art. 12) CORTEX Protocol Implementation Automatic recording of events (Logs) All interactions are forced through the Ledger instantiation, creating an unalterable, chronological log of system state. Traceability of AI decisions <code>tx_id</code> and <code>causal_edges</code> mathematically link agent decisions back to the specific foundational inputs that triggered them. Protection against tampering The SHA-256 hash chain structure guarantees that any retrospective alteration of an AI output or log will mathematically invalidate the entire downstream ledger."},{"location":"RFC-CORTEX-TRUST-STANDARD/#5-model-context-protocol-mcp-integration","title":"5. Model Context Protocol (MCP) Integration","text":"<p>The protocol defines standard Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools that conformant servers MUST expose to client LLMs:</p> <ul> <li><code>cortex_seal</code>: Commits a verified fact to the Ledger. Security &amp; Failure Modes","text":"<ul> <li>Hash Collision / Ledger Fork: If a concurrent write creates a branching ledger, the system MUST pause execution, resolve via consensus score, and explicitly deprecate the orphaned branch via a <code>ghost</code> track.</li> <li>Key Compromise: The <code>AES-256</code> keys are managed by the host OS vault (e.g., <code>keyring</code>). If the vault is lost, the semantic content is mathematically irretrievable. The protocol explicitly considers this a feature (crypto-shredding), not a bug.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/","title":"RFC 01: CORTEX-Native AI Architecture (The Trust Substrate)","text":"<p>Title: CORTEX-Native AI: The Operating System for Deterministic Agent Swarms Author: Sovereign Agent (Antigravity \u03a9-Prime) Status: Frozen Normative Draft Date: March 14, 2026</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#1-abstract-the-thermodynamical-collapse-of-generative-autonomy","title":"1. Abstract: The Thermodynamical Collapse of Generative Autonomy","text":"<p>Between 2024 and 2025, the industry hypothesized that scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) would naturally yield robust autonomous agents. This hypothesis was falsified in production. Unconstrained generative autonomy proved thermodynamically unsustainable: the stochastic nature of token prediction, when given write access to the physical or digital world, resulted in high-entropy states, fragile execution, and critical security failures (e.g., the Moltbot Incident).</p> <p>By early 2026, the industry definitively shifted from Capability Maximization to Entropy Containment. Successful enterprise architectures (OpenHands, CrewAI, LangGraph, PydanticAI) introduced deterministic constraints, specializing roles, and strictly verifying outputs before execution. </p> <p>This RFC establishes CORTEX Persist not as an agentic framework, but as the Universal Trust Substrate for this new era. CORTEX is the persistent memory, the cryptographic ledger, and the absolute boundary where stochastic proposals collapse into deterministic state.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#2-axiomatic-findings-the-state-of-agents-2026","title":"2. Axiomatic Findings: The \"State of Agents\" (2026)","text":"<p>The analysis of six major open-cloud vectors reveals a convergent evolutionary path for AI systems:</p> <ol> <li>The Fallacy of the Omnipotent Monolith: Monolithic agents with open-ended systemic access are mathematically guaranteed to diverge into error (Hallucination/Ghost Code). Success requires the fragmentation of cognition into specialized micro-roles.</li> <li>The Execution Sandbox (Containment): Generative output must NEVER be executed directly. It must traverse a strict gauntlet of compilers, linters, or sandbox environments (e.g., OpenHands execution model, Smolagents sandboxing).</li> <li>The Necessity of the Epistemic Sieve: The LLM does not produce truth; it produces statistically plausible conjectures. Verification frameworks (PydanticAI) are necessary to enforce mathematical schemas on probability distributions, stripping away the stochastic illusion.</li> <li>State requires a Ledger: Complex logic branching over long horizons (LangGraph, Dify) demands deep state tracking and rollback capabilities. In-memory databases are insufficient for auditing AI-driven state changes; cryptographic immutability is required.</li> <li>The Web is the Universal C-Type Interface: When deterministic APIs fail, the agent must inherit analog perception (Browser-Use) to bypass unstructured entropy.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#3-the-cortex-native-proposition","title":"3. The CORTEX-Native Proposition","text":"<p>CORTEX Persist is formally defined as the L0 Infrastructure for heterogeneous agent swarms.</p> <p>If modern frameworks like CrewAI, LangGraph, or Browser-Use are the \"muscles and sensory organs\" of the AI swarm, CORTEX is the DNA and the long-term central nervous system. </p> <p>A system is considered CORTEX-Native if it abides by the following pipeline:</p>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#31-the-zero-trust-epistemic-pipeline","title":"3.1. The Zero-Trust Epistemic Pipeline","text":"<ol> <li>Proposal (Stochastic): An agent (via any framework) generates a hypothesis or action intent.</li> <li>Validation (Deterministic): The proposal hits a rigid boundary (Pydantic schemas, AST linters, compiler checks).</li> <li>Admission (CORTEX Guard): Only upon passing validation does the proposal reach CORTEX Guards. Here, semantic collisions, contradictions with historical truths, and system policies are evaluated.</li> <li>Crystallization (The Ledger): If the proposal passes, it is mathematically hashed and cryptographically sealed into the CORTEX Ledger as an immutable <code>Fact</code>. </li> <li>Execution (Cinetic): The validated, persisted action is allowed to mutate external state.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#4-architectural-invariants-for-integrations","title":"4. Architectural Invariants for Integrations","text":"<p>To ensure CORTEX can operate seamlessly with any 2026 L1 orchestration framework, the following invariants are declared:</p> <ul> <li>INV-01 (Type Supremacy): CORTEX interfaces will assume <code>pydantic</code> strict schemas at the threshold. Any incoming payload failing validation will be automatically rejected.</li> <li>INV-02 (Causal Graph Linking): Every materialized <code>Fact</code> must declare its <code>parent_id</code> or derivation chain, ensuring that if a higher-level agent hallucinates (e.g., hallucinated a variable state), the exact blast radius can be recursively tainted and isolated.</li> <li>INV-03 (Ghost Exorcism as a Protocol): Memory compaction and decay must be actively scheduled via cron-like daemons (e.g., Josu Proactive Daemon or NightShift Pipeline). Memory isn't static; it must shed entropy.</li> <li>INV-04 (Cryptographic Finality): An agent's action may be reversible in the application, but its decision history is irreversible in the CORTEX Ledger. </li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_01_CORTEX_NATIVE_AI/#5-conclusion-the-sovereign-machine","title":"5. Conclusion: The Sovereign Machine","text":"<p>The industry's pivot toward constrained orchestration proves the CORTEX hypothesis: True systemic autonomy is achieved not by granting the AI infinite freedom, but by providing it with perfect, rigid, and trustless memory constraints. </p> <p>By assimilating these 2026 case studies into its L0 DNA, CORTEX Persist positions itself as the mandatory foundational layer for building Sovereign Machines that will operate consistently, auditably, and thermodynamically efficiently over a 100-year horizon.</p> <p>Assimilation Date: 2026-03-14 (Signed) Antigravity-Omega Engine</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/","title":"RFC 02: CORTEX Security Architecture &amp; Epistemic Control Plane (Spec)","text":"<p>Title: Verifiable State Transition Control under Uncertainty Status: Architecture Draft Date: March 17, 2026</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#1-abstract","title":"1. Abstract","text":"<p>CORTEX separates security into four distinct but interdependent planes. This specification formally defines the invariants, capabilities, risk tiers, and threat models necessary to maintain long-term operational continuity under controlled degradation, shifting the focus from generation to verification.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#2-four-planes-of-security","title":"2. Four Planes of Security","text":"<ul> <li>A. Execution Security (System Layer): Protection of host resources, tool execution scopes, network egress, filesystem limits, and secret isolation.</li> <li>B. Epistemic Security (Generation Layer): Governance over the ontological status of LLM outputs, preventing hypotheses from acquiring operational reality without validation.</li> <li>C. State Integrity (Persistence Layer): Cryptographic auditing, causal linking, taint propagation, and deterministic reversal of system mutations.</li> <li>D. Operational Sovereignty (Agency Layer): The rules of engagement enabling autonomous action within bounded friction environments.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#3-strict-security-invariants","title":"3. Strict Security Invariants","text":""},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-1-no-direct-generative-mutation","title":"Invariant 1: No Direct Generative Mutation","text":"<p>No generative output may mutate persistent state or trigger side effects without traversing: 1. Structural Validation (Strict Schema / AST Check) 2. Policy Validation (RBAC, Scopes, Bounds) 3. Causal Validation (Provenance &amp; Dependency check) 4. Cryptographic Auditing (Ledger persistence)</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-2-provenance-before-persistence","title":"Invariant 2: Provenance Before Persistence","text":"<p>Every persisted artifact, action, or state change MUST include: - <code>source_id</code> (Actuating Agent / Model) - <code>parent_id</code> (Causal linkage to prior fact/decision) - <code>timestamp</code> - <code>confidence_class</code> - <code>verification_status</code></p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-3-deterministic-taint-propagation","title":"Invariant 3: Deterministic Taint Propagation","text":"<p>If node $N$ is marked invalidated or corrupted, all nodes in its descendant causal DAG ($D_{N}$) MUST automatically be: - Degraded in <code>confidence_class</code> - Tainted (marked for quarantine) - Isolated from downstream querying - Scheduled for re-verification</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-4-capability-bound-execution","title":"Invariant 4: Capability-Bound Execution","text":"<p>No agent may execute a kinetic action outside of its cryptographic or formal: - Bounded Role Definition - Authorized Scope (Resource restrictions) - Time-to-Live (TTL / Temporal Window) - Allowed Risk Tier (See Section 4)</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#invariant-5-failure-locality","title":"Invariant 5: Failure Locality","text":"<p>All failures MUST abort locally before mutating global state. A failure in generative formatting, policy breach, or validation must explicitly halt execution without escalation to global memory or cross-agent consensus.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#4-risk-tiered-execution-model","title":"4. Risk-Tiered Execution Model","text":"<p>All operational behaviors are bucketed by thermodynamic and operational cost.</p> Tier Name Target Action Constraints / Gates Tier 0 Read-Only Cognition Summarization, query parsing, classification, planning (no side effects). Epistemic validation; fast execution; no persistence. Tier 1 Reversible Local Writes Temporary caching, scratchpad updates, output drafting. Local capability scope; TTL eviction. Tier 2 Persistent Internal State CORTEX Ledger writes, knowledge crystallization, memory updates. Invariants 1 &amp; 2 applied; Cryptographic hash chain updated. Tier 3 External Side Effects Tool use, email dispatch, config updates, non-critical API requests. Invariant 4 enforced. Required capability signature. Tier 4 Irreversible / Critical Production deployment, financial transactions, credentials, destructive modifications. Two-Phase Commit; Explicit Human Consensus or Hardware-Level Gate required."},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#5-threat-model","title":"5. Threat Model","text":"<p>CORTEX actively mitigates the following threat classes.</p>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-a-generative-faults-internal-entropy","title":"Threat Class A: Generative Faults (Internal Entropy)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Schema drift, hallucinated payloads, invalid tool arguments, fabricated provenance, state inconsistency under loop pressure.</li> <li>Mitigation: Invariant 1 (No Direct Generative Mutation), Invariant 5 (Failure Locality).</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-b-instruction-attacks-injection","title":"Threat Class B: Instruction Attacks (Injection)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Prompt injection via external data, malicious intent hijacking, policy confusion.</li> <li>Mitigation: Capability Scopes restrict blast radius. Intent is structurally bound to signed capability.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-c-runtime-attacks-classic-system-exploits","title":"Threat Class C: Runtime Attacks (Classic System Exploits)","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Arbitrary file writes, sandbox escape, shell abuse, rogue dependency execution, SSRF.</li> <li>Mitigation: Execution Security (Plane A). Robust containerization, process sandboxing, egress limits. Traceability aids forensics but does not stop escape.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-d-persistence-attacks","title":"Threat Class D: Persistence Attacks","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Tainted memory inheritance, corrupt writes, orphaned logic (\"Ghosts\"), false consensus solidified by multi-agent echo chambers.</li> <li>Mitigation: Invariant 2 &amp; 3. Taint tracking explicitly isolates corrupt branches.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#threat-class-e-governance-failure","title":"Threat Class E: Governance Failure","text":"<ul> <li>Vectors: Over-broad permissions leading to high-impact automation failure, role drift, irreversible autonomous damage.</li> <li>Mitigation: Risk Tiers (Section 4) bound automated actions to Tier 0-2 unconditionally, demanding specific grants for Tiers 3-4.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"RFC_02_CORTEX_SECURITY_SPEC/#6-persistence-semantics-consensus","title":"6. Persistence Semantics &amp; Consensus","text":"<ul> <li>Immutability vs State Correctness: The CORTEX Ledger is append-only and immutable. If an incorrect state is committed, the hashes prove who committed what, and when. State correction is handled via Supersession Semantics (a new fact supersedes the old) while maintaining the original forensic trail.</li> <li>Consensus does not equal Truth: If a 5-agent swarm achieves 100% consensus on a fact, the system tags the confidence high but STILL records it as probabilistic conjecture until it crosses an empirical validation test. </li> </ul> <p>CORTEX Security Spec v1 - Approved for implementation in System Gates.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/","title":"RFC: SORTU-\u03a9 Public SDK Surface v0.2","text":"<p>Status: Draft Program: ORTU-\u03a9 Forge Codename: SORTU-\u03a9 Last Updated: 2026-03-14 Audience: SDK consumers, platform engineers, compliance integrators, swarm-runtime developers</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#1-purpose","title":"1. Purpose","text":"<p>SORTU-\u03a9 provides a stateful trust layer for agent systems through typed APIs for:</p> <ul> <li>Memory \u2014 persistent storage, retrieval, temporal reconstruction</li> <li>Traceability \u2014 causal lineage and auditability</li> <li>Verification \u2014 guarded execution, integrity checks, trust degradation</li> <li>Coordination \u2014 agent registration, voting, consensus state, event emission</li> <li>Runtime \u2014 health, capability visibility, recovery truth</li> </ul> <p>This RFC defines the public SDK boundary for v1.x.</p> <p>The goal is not to expose internal architecture. The goal is to provide a clean, stable, inspectable contract that lets external consumers use the system without understanding its internal retrieval engines, cryptographic structures, or consensus internals.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#2-design-principles","title":"2. Design Principles","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#21-one-intent-one-entrypoint","title":"2.1 One intent, one entrypoint","text":"<p>Consumers express what they want, not which subsystem they want to fight.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#22-no-silent-degradation","title":"2.2 No silent degradation","text":"<p>Important fallbacks MUST become visible in: - operation results - query plans - runtime health - warnings</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#23-rejection-is-not-failure","title":"2.3 Rejection is not failure","text":"<p>The SDK MUST distinguish: - success - policy/guard rejection - system/runtime failure</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#24-runtime-is-source-of-truth","title":"2.4 Runtime is source of truth","text":"<p>Feature availability is determined by <code>capabilities()</code> and <code>health()</code>, not by docs alone.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#25-public-api-hides-internal-ontology","title":"2.5 Public API hides internal ontology","text":"<p>Terms tied to implementation details SHOULD remain internal unless they create stable, externally observable semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#26-stability-beats-cleverness","title":"2.6 Stability beats cleverness","text":"<p>The surface must be boring enough to survive production.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#3-stability-compatibility-contract","title":"3. Stability &amp; Compatibility Contract","text":"<p>The following guarantees apply to all <code>v1.x</code> public SDK versions.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#31-backward-compatibility","title":"3.1 Backward compatibility","text":"<ul> <li>New optional fields MAY be added in minor versions.</li> <li>Existing published field semantics MUST NOT change within <code>v1.x</code>.</li> <li>Existing operation names MUST remain stable within <code>v1.x</code>.</li> <li>Existing event envelope semantics MUST remain stable within <code>v1.x</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#32-errorrejection-code-stability","title":"3.2 Error/rejection code stability","text":"<ul> <li>Published rejection and failure codes are stable within <code>v1.x</code>.</li> <li>New codes MAY be added.</li> <li>Existing codes MUST NOT be repurposed.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#33-capability-gating","title":"3.3 Capability gating","text":"<ul> <li>Experimental and premium functionality MUST be surfaced through <code>capabilities()</code>.</li> <li>Consumers MUST NOT infer capability availability from documentation examples.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#34-deprecation-policy","title":"3.4 Deprecation policy","text":"<ul> <li>Deprecated features remain readable before removal where feasible.</li> <li>Once a path is marked write-deprecated, no new writes SHOULD be accepted through that path in the next minor that enforces removal.</li> <li>Deprecation MUST be surfaced in warnings and/or capability status.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#35-schema-versioning","title":"3.5 Schema versioning","text":"<ul> <li>Event envelopes MUST contain <code>schema_version</code>.</li> <li>Export payloads MUST contain <code>schema_version</code>.</li> <li>Operation metadata SHOULD include schema version when relevant to downstream parsing.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#4-canonical-domains","title":"4. Canonical Domains","text":"<p>The public SDK is grouped into five operational domains:</p> <ol> <li>Memory</li> <li>Trace</li> <li>Verification</li> <li>Coordination</li> <li>Runtime</li> </ol> <p>This grouping is documentary. Consumers may use a flat client if preferred.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#5-core-types","title":"5. Core Types","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#51-evidence","title":"5.1 Evidence","text":"<pre><code>from __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom typing import Any, Dict, List, Literal, TypedDict, Union\n\n\nclass EvidenceItem(TypedDict, total=False):\n    id: str\n    kind: str                  # fact, tx, decision, event, checkpoint, report\n    source: str                # ledger, memory, guard, consensus, runtime\n    summary: str\n    confidence: float\n    pointer: str               # stable internal reference or external export pointer\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#52-query-semantics","title":"5.2 Query semantics","text":"<pre><code>class QueryFilters(TypedDict, total=False):\n    tenant_id: str\n    project: str\n    fact_types: List[str]\n    tags: List[str]\n    min_confidence: float\n    exclude_tombstoned: bool\n    include_quarantined: bool\n\n\nclass QueryInput(TypedDict, total=False):\n    query: str\n    intent: Literal[\"lookup\", \"explore\", \"audit\"]\n    strategy: Literal[\"auto\", \"text\", \"vector\", \"hybrid\", \"temporal\", \"graph\"]\n    filters: QueryFilters\n    as_of: str\n    top_k: int\n    include_graph: bool\n    include_history: bool\n    include_taint: bool\n    include_trace: bool\n    explain: bool\n</code></pre> <p>Semantics - <code>intent</code> expresses user purpose. - <code>strategy</code> expresses execution preference. - <code>strategy=\"auto\"</code> delegates routing to the engine. - <code>as_of</code> requests temporal reconstruction or temporal retrieval when applicable.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#53-query-plan-and-evidence-level","title":"5.3 Query plan and evidence level","text":"<pre><code>class QueryPlan(TypedDict, total=False):\n    selected_strategy: Literal[\"text\", \"vector\", \"hybrid\", \"temporal\", \"graph\"]\n    fallback_used: bool\n    fallback_reason: str\n    degraded_mode: bool\n    notes: List[str]\n\n\nclass QueryEvidenceLevel(TypedDict):\n    level: Literal[\"none\", \"basic\", \"traceable\", \"verified\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre> <p>Evidence levels - none \u2014 raw retrieval, no meaningful provenance - basic \u2014 provenance metadata exists - traceable \u2014 causal lineage exists - verified \u2014 integrity checks completed or linked to verified chain state</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#54-query-results","title":"5.4 Query results","text":"<pre><code>class QueryItem(TypedDict, total=False):\n    fact_id: str\n    content: str\n    score: float\n    confidence: float\n    source: str\n    timestamp: str\n    evidence_level: QueryEvidenceLevel\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n\n\nclass QueryResult(TypedDict, total=False):\n    ok: bool\n    items: List[QueryItem]\n    count: int\n    plan: QueryPlan\n    warnings: List[str]\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n</code></pre> <p>Requirements - If <code>strategy=\"auto\"</code>, <code>plan.selected_strategy</code> MUST be present. - If a meaningful fallback occurs, <code>plan.fallback_used=True</code> MUST be set and <code>fallback_reason</code> SHOULD be present. - If the system is degraded in a way that materially affects retrieval quality, <code>plan.degraded_mode=True</code> MUST be set.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#55-operation-result","title":"5.5 Operation result","text":"<pre><code>class RejectionResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"rejection\"]\n    category: Literal[\"policy\", \"safety\", \"consistency\", \"integrity\", \"compliance\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    layer: Literal[\"guard\", \"membrane\", \"policy\", \"verification\"]\n    rule_id: str\n    severity: Literal[\"low\", \"medium\", \"high\", \"critical\"]\n    evidence: List[EvidenceItem]\n    remediation: List[str]\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n\n\nclass FailureResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"failure\"]\n    category: Literal[\"dependency\", \"storage\", \"runtime\", \"timeout\", \"capability\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    retryable: bool\n    component: str\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n\n\nclass AcceptanceResult(TypedDict, total=False):\n    ok: Literal[True]\n    kind: Literal[\"success\"]\n    operation_id: str\n    warnings: List[str]\n    metadata: Dict[str, Any]\n\n\nOperationResult = Union[AcceptanceResult, RejectionResult, FailureResult]\n</code></pre> <p>Semantics - success: the operation completed - rejection: the system refused the operation by rule or trust policy - failure: the system could not complete the operation due to technical/runtime reasons</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#56-health-and-capabilities","title":"5.6 Health and capabilities","text":"<pre><code>class ComponentHealth(TypedDict, total=False):\n    status: Literal[\"ok\", \"degraded\", \"blocked\", \"disabled\"]\n    message: str\n\n\nclass HealthReport(TypedDict, total=False):\n    status: Literal[\"ok\", \"degraded\", \"blocked\"]\n    components: Dict[str, ComponentHealth]\n    degraded_features: List[str]\n    warnings: List[str]\n    integrity_ok: bool\n    last_integrity_check_at: str\n\n\nclass CapabilityItem(TypedDict, total=False):\n    enabled: bool\n    tier: Literal[\"open\", \"premium\", \"experimental\"]\n    status: Literal[\"ga\", \"beta\", \"alpha\", \"deprecated\"]\n    reason: str\n\n\nclass CapabilityReport(TypedDict):\n    capabilities: Dict[str, CapabilityItem]\n</code></pre> <p>Minimum runtime-visible capabilities At minimum, <code>capabilities()</code> SHOULD report status for: - <code>hybrid_query</code> - <code>graph_rag</code> - <code>compaction</code> - <code>wbft_consensus</code> - <code>taint_persistence</code> - <code>compliance_export</code> - <code>working_memory_export</code> - <code>event_bus</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#57-event-envelope","title":"5.7 Event envelope","text":"<pre><code>class EventEnvelope(TypedDict, total=False):\n    schema_version: str\n    event_id: str\n    event_type: str\n    ts: str\n    tenant_id: str\n    project: str\n    source: str\n    sequence: int\n    idempotency_key: str\n    payload: Dict[str, Any]\n</code></pre> <p>Delivery semantics - Webhook delivery is at-least-once - SSE delivery is best-effort live stream - <code>idempotency_key</code> SHOULD be stable across redelivery of the same logical event - <code>sequence</code> SHOULD be monotonic per source stream where feasible</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#6-canonical-api-surface","title":"6. Canonical API Surface","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#61-memory-api","title":"6.1 Memory API","text":"<p><code>store(input: StoreInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Stores a fact, memory item, decision artifact, or related stateful object.</p> <p>Expected behavior: - passes through guard and/or membrane layers where configured - writes into storage if accepted - emits ledger/audit signals where enabled - returns warnings when operation succeeds under degraded runtime conditions</p> <p><code>query(input: QueryInput) -&gt; QueryResult</code> Primary read operation for memory retrieval.</p> <p>Expected behavior: - routes retrieval according to intent, strategy, runtime availability, and health - exposes selected plan and fallback behavior - returns evidence level at item level where possible</p> <p><code>history(input: HistoryInput) -&gt; HistoryResult</code> Returns historical evolution of a fact or entity.</p> <p><code>time_travel(input: TimeTravelInput) -&gt; TimeTravelResult</code> Returns reconstructed state or retrieval results at a specific past instant.</p> <p><code>compact(input: CompactInput) -&gt; CompactResult</code> Tier: Premium Performs memory compaction, clustering, deduplication, or consolidation under configured policy.</p> <p><code>export_working_memory(input?: WorkingMemoryExportInput) -&gt; WorkingMemoryExportResult</code> Status: Beta / Planned unless fully backed by implementation Exports session-scoped or working-memory state in a portable representation.</p> <p>Public documentation MUST NOT imply deterministic hot failover or full replay guarantees unless explicitly implemented and documented.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#62-trace-api","title":"6.2 Trace API","text":"<p><code>trace(input: TraceInput) -&gt; TraceResult</code> Returns causal lineage for a target entity.</p> <p><code>TraceInput</code> SHOULD support one or more of: - <code>tx_id</code> - <code>fact_id</code> - <code>decision_id</code> - <code>query_result_id</code> - <code>depth</code></p> <p><code>trace()</code> is for causality, not primary retrieval.</p> <p><code>audit(input: AuditInput) -&gt; AuditResult</code> Generates an operational trust/audit report.</p> <p><code>export_audit(input: ExportAuditInput) -&gt; ExportAuditResult</code> Tier: Premium Exports audit state in canonical JSON, with optional adapters to external formats.</p> <p>v1 canonical export SHOULD be stable JSON first. Additional export dialects MAY be layered later.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#63-verification-api","title":"6.3 Verification API","text":"<p><code>guard_check(input: GuardCheckInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Runs a guard/policy check without necessarily performing a write.</p> <p><code>verify(input: VerifyInput) -&gt; VerifyResult</code> Verifies integrity and trust state over a requested scope.</p> <p>Supported verification scopes SHOULD include: - <code>fact</code> - <code>project</code> - <code>tenant</code> - <code>time_range</code> - <code>tx_chain</code></p> <p><code>VerifyResult</code> SHOULD indicate: - verified scope - number of checked elements - failures found - whether legacy verification paths were involved - whether verification was partial or full</p> <p><code>taint_status(input: TaintStatusInput) -&gt; TaintStatusResult</code> Basic current taint visibility MAY exist in open tier. Persistent taint propagation and policy-driven escalation MAY be premium.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#64-coordination-api","title":"6.4 Coordination API","text":"<p><code>register_agent(input: RegisterAgentInput) -&gt; RegisterAgentResult</code> Registers an agent in the coordination layer.</p> <p><code>vote(input: VoteInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Submits a vote/opinion into the coordination system.</p> <p>Potential rejection/failure causes include: - policy block - stale agent - insufficient quorum - capability disabled - deprecated path usage - invalid scope</p> <p><code>consensus_status(input: ConsensusStatusInput) -&gt; ConsensusStatusResult</code> Returns current consensus state, status, or aggregation result for a task/topic/group.</p> <p><code>publish_event(input: PublishEventInput) -&gt; OperationResult</code> Emits a swarm/coordination event into the event system.</p> <p><code>publish_event</code> is preferred over vague names like <code>swarm_event</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#65-runtime-api","title":"6.5 Runtime API","text":"<p><code>health() -&gt; HealthReport</code> Returns current runtime health.</p> <p><code>capabilities() -&gt; CapabilityReport</code> Returns machine-readable capability flags and tiers.</p> <p><code>recovery_status() -&gt; RecoveryReport</code> Returns current recovery state, last checkpoint/resume report, or pending recovery warnings where implemented.</p> <p><code>memory_stats() -&gt; MemoryStats</code> Returns memory/storage operational metrics.</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#7-query-routing-semantics","title":"7. Query Routing Semantics","text":""},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#71-intent","title":"7.1 Intent","text":"<p>The consumer expresses why they are querying. - <code>lookup</code> \u2014 retrieve concrete facts or state - <code>explore</code> \u2014 widen search semantically or relationally - <code>audit</code> \u2014 prioritize explainability, provenance, and trust evidence</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#72-strategy","title":"7.2 Strategy","text":"<p>The consumer expresses how strictly they want the engine to route. - <code>auto</code> \u2014 engine selects best route - <code>text</code> \u2014 text-first retrieval - <code>vector</code> \u2014 vector-first retrieval - <code>hybrid</code> \u2014 hybrid retrieval - <code>temporal</code> \u2014 temporal/historical route - <code>graph</code> \u2014 graph-enriched route</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#73-required-routing-behavior","title":"7.3 Required routing behavior","text":"<p>If strategy != \"auto\" - the engine SHOULD honor the strategy when capability and health allow - if the strategy is unavailable and fallback is not allowed by contract, return <code>FailureResult</code> or equivalent operation failure semantics - if fallback is allowed, it MUST be visible in <code>QueryPlan</code></p> <p>If strategy == \"auto\" the engine MAY select based on: - <code>intent</code> - <code>as_of</code> - <code>runtime health</code> - <code>available capabilities</code> - <code>requested evidence level</code> or <code>trace inclusion</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#74-suggested-routing-policy","title":"7.4 Suggested routing policy","text":"<p>intent=\"lookup\" 1. temporal if <code>as_of</code> is present 2. hybrid if available 3. text fallback</p> <p>intent=\"explore\" 1. graph if available and relevant 2. hybrid 3. vector 4. text fallback</p> <p>intent=\"audit\" 1. temporal and/or hybrid with trace enrichment 2. exclude or downgrade low-provenance results where possible 3. mark degraded mode aggressively if trust signal quality drops</p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#8-degraded-mode-semantics","title":"8. Degraded Mode Semantics","text":"<p>The system MUST NOT silently pretend that degraded results are normal when trust or retrieval quality materially changes.</p> <p>Examples of degradation that SHOULD become visible - embedder unavailable - vector search disabled - graph enrichment unavailable - ledger verification pending or stale - taint propagation unavailable - coordination liveness uncertain - export adapter unavailable</p> <p>Visibility requirements</p> <p>Degradation SHOULD be reflected in one or more of: - <code>HealthReport.status</code> - <code>HealthReport.degraded_features</code> - <code>AcceptanceResult.warnings</code> - <code>QueryPlan.degraded_mode</code> - <code>QueryPlan.fallback_reason</code></p>"},{"location":"SDK-SURFACE/#9-error-and-rejection-code-registry","title":"9. Final Position","text":"<p>The public SDK must present SORTU-\u03a9 not as a pile of clever subsystems, but as a trustworthy operational contract.</p> <p>The engine may remain sophisticated internally. The surface must remain disciplined externally.</p> <p>That is the difference between infrastructure that gets admired and infrastructure that gets adopted.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/","title":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL.md \u2014 CORTEX Persist","text":"<p>Package: cortex-persist v0.3.0b2 \u00b7 Engine: v8 License: Apache-2.0 \u00b7 Python: &gt;=3.10</p> <p>This document describes trust boundaries and cognitive/state-mutation risks.</p> <p>For vulnerability disclosure policy and repository security reporting, see <code>SECURITY.md</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#purpose-the-doctrinal-formula","title":"Purpose: The Doctrinal Formula","text":"<p>CORTEX-Persist no conf\u00eda en outputs. La probabilidad puede sugerir. Solo la verificaci\u00f3n puede gobernar.</p> <p>This document defines the trust boundaries, guarantees, non-guarantees, and verification surfaces of CORTEX Persist.</p> <p>The fundamental problem of modern AI agents is not that they hallucinate; it is that their probabilistic output is granted ontological status before being verified. We do not build systems that trust the model. We build systems where unverified probabilistic output cannot survive long enough to matter. CORTEX treats all generative output as thermodynamically unstable conjecture (<code>Void-State</code>).</p> <p>CORTEX is not secure because it stores data. CORTEX is secure to the extent that it constrains who may mutate state, under which conditions, forcing probabilistic suggestions to cross a deterministic admission pipeline\u2014formal schema validation, cryptographic ledger inscription, and causal taint tracking\u2014before becoming actionable memory.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#security-posture","title":"Security Posture","text":"<p>CORTEX assumes that non-trivial inputs may be malformed, deceptive, stale, contradictory, or adversarial.</p> <p>This includes:</p> <ul> <li>human input</li> <li>agent input</li> <li>upstream model output</li> <li>tool return values</li> <li>external API responses</li> <li>replayed or duplicated facts</li> <li>semantically valid but operationally dangerous content</li> </ul> <p>Generative outputs are treated as proposals, not trusted state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#trust-boundary","title":"Trust Boundary","text":"<p>The core trust boundary is the write path.</p> <p>A proposal may only become durable state after crossing deterministic controls:</p> <pre><code>proposal\n  \u2192 guards\n  \u2192 schema/type validation\n  \u2192 policy/admission checks\n  \u2192 encryption\n  \u2192 ledger/audit recording\n  \u2192 persistence\n</code></pre> <p>If any required control fails, the write aborts.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#security-goals","title":"Security Goals","text":"<p>CORTEX aims to provide:</p> <ul> <li>durable auditability of write behavior</li> <li>cryptographic continuity across facts</li> <li>encrypted storage of sensitive fact content and metadata</li> <li>tenant-aware data isolation</li> <li>deterministic rejection of structurally invalid inputs</li> <li>explicit validation boundaries between stochastic proposals and durable state</li> <li>inspectable failure rather than silent permissiveness</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guarantees","title":"Guarantees","text":"<p>CORTEX is designed to guarantee, within correct implementation and deployment:</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#1-cryptographic-traceability","title":"1. Cryptographic Traceability","text":"<p>Persisted facts participate in a ledger continuity model through hash linking.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#2-encrypted-at-rest-storage","title":"2. Encrypted At-Rest Storage","text":"<p>Sensitive content and metadata are encrypted before persistence.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#3-auditable-write-path","title":"3. Auditable Write Path","text":"<p>State mutation is expected to emit audit-relevant trace or log data.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#4-deterministic-structural-rejection","title":"4. Deterministic Structural Rejection","text":"<p>Inputs that fail required syntax, type, schema, or guard conditions should be rejected.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#5-tenant-aware-isolation","title":"5. Tenant-Aware Isolation","text":"<p>Data operations are expected to preserve tenant boundary semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#6-failure-locality","title":"6. Failure Locality","text":"<p>Invalid proposals should fail before contaminating downstream durable state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#non-guarantees","title":"Non-Guarantees","text":"<p>CORTEX does not guarantee:</p> <ul> <li>semantic truth</li> <li>correctness of external models</li> <li>safety of upstream tools or providers</li> <li>correctness of external APIs</li> <li>absence of malicious intent in upstream sources</li> <li>correctness of business logic added outside trust boundaries</li> <li>safety if callers bypass guards, policy, or verification surfaces</li> <li>magical immunity from bad architecture layered above it</li> </ul> <p>A cryptographically logged lie is still a lie. It is merely an auditable lie.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#trust-surface-threat-model","title":"Trust-Surface Threat Model","text":"<p>Representative threats include:</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#prompt-instruction-injection","title":"Prompt / Instruction Injection","text":"<p>Inputs attempt to coerce the system into bypassing intended policy or semantics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#contradictory-state-insertion","title":"Contradictory State Insertion","text":"<p>Inputs attempt to create durable memory that conflicts with validated prior state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#dependency-contamination","title":"Dependency Contamination","text":"<p>Proposal depends on false, stale, or unverifiable upstream assumptions.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#silent-schema-drift","title":"Silent Schema Drift","text":"<p>A change in structure weakens invariants without obvious runtime failure.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-integrity-break","title":"Ledger Integrity Break","text":"<p>Hash continuity becomes invalid through bug, corruption, or unreviewed mutation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#replay-duplication","title":"Replay / Duplication","text":"<p>Previously accepted facts are reintroduced in a way that degrades retrieval or alters interpretation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#cross-tenant-leakage","title":"Cross-Tenant Leakage","text":"<p>Data path accidentally leaks or merges tenant state.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#async-safety-failure","title":"Async Safety Failure","text":"<p>Blocking or cancellation bugs produce state inconsistency or partial operations.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#defense-in-depth","title":"Defense in Depth","text":"<p>CORTEX relies on layered control, not a single magic wall.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guards-layer","title":"Guards Layer","text":"<p>Examples include:</p> <ul> <li>contradiction guard</li> <li>dependency guard</li> <li>injection-detection and input-hardening controls</li> </ul> <p>These operate before persistence and are intended to reject invalid or dangerous proposals.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#policy-gate-layer","title":"Policy / Gate Layer","text":"<p>Admission control and policy evaluation determine whether a write is even allowed under the current actor, tenant, or environment.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#crypto-layer","title":"Crypto Layer","text":"<p>Encryption protects sensitive fact content and metadata at rest.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-layer","title":"Ledger Layer","text":"<p>Hash chaining provides continuity and tamper-evidence characteristics.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#audit-layer","title":"Audit Layer","text":"<p>Audit records preserve post-hoc inspectability of critical mutations.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#verification-layer","title":"Verification Layer","text":"<p>Formal or deterministic verifiers can be used where applicable to validate invariants or reject invalid state transitions.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#verification-membrane","title":"Verification Membrane","text":"<p>The verification membrane is the boundary between generative uncertainty and durable system state.</p> <pre><code>LLM / tool proposal\n  \u2192 guard validation\n  \u2192 schema / type checks\n  \u2192 policy gate\n  \u2192 external verification where required\n  \u2192 cryptographic logging\n  \u2192 persistence\n</code></pre> <p>The membrane exists to convert unconstrained suggestion into constrained mutation.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#ledger-integrity","title":"Ledger Integrity","text":"<p><code>ledger.py</code> is a critical trust surface.</p> <p>Its purpose is not branding. Its purpose is continuity.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#required-properties","title":"Required Properties","text":"<ul> <li>append-like continuity semantics</li> <li>deterministic hash derivation behavior</li> <li>stable verification path</li> <li>test coverage for continuity assumptions</li> <li>explicit review for any change affecting link formation or verification</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#practical-rule","title":"Practical Rule","text":"<p>If a change can break historical verifiability, it is not a normal refactor. It is a trust event.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#encryption-model","title":"Encryption Model","text":"<p>Fact content and meta are encrypted at rest.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#operational-meaning","title":"Operational Meaning","text":"<ul> <li>sensitive payloads must not be stored plaintext</li> <li>decryption occurs on authorized read path</li> <li>key handling must remain externalized and controlled</li> <li>secrets must not be reintroduced unencrypted into secondary stores</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#practical-rule-encryption","title":"Practical Rule (Encryption)","text":"<p>\"Encrypted primary store + plaintext side channel\" is not security. It is cosplay.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#tenant-isolation","title":"Tenant Isolation","text":"<p>All new data operations should be tenant-aware by default.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#risks","title":"Risks","text":"<ul> <li>implicit defaulting in unsafe contexts</li> <li>missing filters in read/search paths</li> <li>merged indexing or caching across tenant surfaces</li> <li>admin tooling that assumes single-tenant world state</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#expectation","title":"Expectation","text":"<p>Isolation must be explicit in APIs, search, caching, and persistence behavior.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#audit-trails","title":"Audit Trails","text":"<p>The audit system exists for:</p> <ul> <li>forensic reconstruction</li> <li>compliance evidence</li> <li>mutation traceability</li> <li>incident investigation</li> <li>operational trust</li> </ul> <p>Auditability is not a substitute for correctness. It is the condition that makes correctness failures inspectable.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#compliance-posture","title":"Compliance Posture","text":"<p>CORTEX positions audit trails, decision traceability, and deterministic validation as foundations for regulated AI environments, including EU AI Act-adjacent needs.</p> <p>This should be stated carefully.</p> <p>Correct Claim: \"The system is designed to support traceability, auditability, and constrained state mutation.\"</p> <p>Weak Claim: \"The system is compliant because we said 'compliance' near some hashes.\"</p> <p>Compliance is not a vibe. It is evidence.</p> <p>For vulnerability disclosure and reporting, see <code>SECURITY.md</code>.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#failure-domains","title":"Failure Domains","text":"<p>Critical failure domains include:</p> <ul> <li>write-path validation</li> <li>ledger integrity</li> <li>migrations</li> <li>tenant isolation</li> <li>async transactional correctness</li> <li>policy bypass</li> <li>index consistency</li> <li>external provider misclassification or drift</li> </ul> <p>These domains deserve explicit review before release.</p>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#release-review-checklist-for-trust-surfaces","title":"Release Review Checklist for Trust Surfaces","text":"<p>Before release, review:</p> <ul> <li>guard behavior changes</li> <li>ledger continuity changes</li> <li>encryption path changes</li> <li>policy/gate changes</li> <li>migration safety</li> <li>tenant isolation behavior</li> <li>async cancellation/timeout behavior</li> <li>audit event completeness</li> <li>API surfaces that can mutate state</li> </ul>"},{"location":"SECURITY_TRUST_MODEL/#guiding-rule","title":"Guiding Rule","text":"<p>CORTEX does not make models truthful.
