Purpose","text":"<p>This document defines the trust semantics that govern SORTU-\u03a9 public behavior.</p> <p><code>SDK-SURFACE.md</code> defines the shape of the API. This document defines the meaning of its outputs.</p> <p>It specifies:</p> <ul> <li>evidence levels</li> <li>degraded mode semantics</li> <li>rejection vs failure semantics</li> <li>integrity semantics</li> <li>taint semantics</li> <li>trust expectations for retrieval, write, verification, and coordination flows</li> </ul> <p>The goal is simple:</p> <p>the system must not merely produce results; it must make their trust posture legible.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#2-trust-model","title":"2. Trust Model","text":"<p>SORTU-\u03a9 is not a generic retrieval engine. It is a stateful trust layer.</p> <p>Trust is represented through composition of:</p> <ol> <li>provenance \u2014 where a result came from</li> <li>causality \u2014 how it came to exist</li> <li>integrity \u2014 whether its surrounding chain is structurally valid</li> <li>policy posture \u2014 whether it passed applicable rules</li> <li>taint posture \u2014 whether error or contamination risk has propagated into it</li> <li>runtime posture \u2014 whether the system is operating in normal or degraded conditions</li> </ol> <p>Trust is never binary in the public surface. It is exposed as a set of machine-readable signals.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#3-foundational-principles","title":"3. Foundational Principles","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#31-no-false-confidence","title":"3.1 No false confidence","text":"<p>The system MUST NOT present degraded, weakly sourced, or weakly verified results as if they were equivalent to fully traceable or verified results.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#32-silence-is-not-neutrality","title":"3.2 Silence is not neutrality","text":"<p>If important retrieval, policy, or verification signals are missing, that absence SHOULD be surfaced as reduced trust, not hidden as normalcy.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#33-rejection-is-informative","title":"3.3 Rejection is informative","text":"<p>A rejected operation is still a successful act of governance if the rejection is intentional, explainable, and machine-readable.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#34-failure-is-operational-not-moral","title":"3.4 Failure is operational, not moral","text":"<p>A component outage or dependency failure is not a policy decision. It MUST be represented as a technical failure, not disguised as rejection.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#35-evidence-is-cumulative","title":"3.5 Evidence is cumulative","text":"<p>Trust increases when multiple dimensions align: - provenance exists - lineage exists - integrity holds - taint is absent or low - runtime is healthy</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#36-degradation-must-be-visible","title":"3.6 Degradation must be visible","text":"<p>If runtime degradation materially changes retrieval quality, verification power, or governance enforcement, public outputs MUST reflect it.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#4-trust-dimensions","title":"4. Trust Dimensions","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#41-provenance","title":"4.1 Provenance","text":"<p>Provenance answers: - where did this item come from? - what subsystem produced or stored it? - what identifiers anchor it?</p> <p>Minimum provenance may include: - source subsystem - fact/tx/decision identifier - timestamp - tenant/project scope</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#42-causality","title":"4.2 Causality","text":"<p>Causality answers: - what prior decision, write, or event led to this item? - what parent or predecessor relationship exists? - can lineage be traversed?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#43-integrity","title":"4.3 Integrity","text":"<p>Integrity answers: - is the relevant chain structurally intact? - does the system have evidence that ledger/hash/Merkle checks passed? - was verification full, partial, or unavailable?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#44-policy-posture","title":"4.4 Policy posture","text":"<p>Policy posture answers: - was the operation or item accepted under current rules? - was it blocked, quarantined, downgraded, or passed?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#45-taint-posture","title":"4.5 Taint posture","text":"<p>Taint posture answers: - is the item downstream from known contradictions, failures, or suspect parents? - has trust been downgraded through propagation?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#46-runtime-posture","title":"4.6 Runtime posture","text":"<p>Runtime posture answers: - was the system healthy when the operation occurred? - were required capabilities available? - were important fallbacks triggered?</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#5-evidence-levels","title":"5. Evidence Levels","text":"<p>Evidence levels communicate the minimum trust visibility attached to a result.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#51-canonical-levels","title":"5.1 Canonical levels","text":"<pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass QueryEvidenceLevel(TypedDict):\n    level: Literal[\"none\", \"basic\", \"traceable\", \"verified\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#52-semantics","title":"5.2 Semantics","text":"<p>none The result exists, but the public surface cannot attach meaningful provenance or trust evidence.</p> <p>Typical cases: - raw text match - minimal metadata - transient or weakly anchored retrieval - degraded subsystem with no lineage available</p> <p>This level SHOULD be treated as informational, not strongly trusted.</p> <p>basic The result has basic provenance and metadata but no strong causal chain.</p> <p>Typical cases: - source and timestamp known - stable fact ID exists - no trace expansion requested or available - integrity state not attached</p> <p>This level is usable for general retrieval but weak for audit or compliance workflows.</p> <p>traceable The result can be linked to causal lineage.</p> <p>Typical cases: - causal chain exists - trace ID or parent decision link exists - provenance is stable enough to follow backward</p> <p>This is the minimum serious level for trust-centric workflows.</p> <p>verified The result is linked to or embedded in verified integrity state.</p> <p>Typical cases: - ledger/hash/Merkle checks passed - verification completed recently enough to be relevant - chain state is structurally valid - evidence is not merely present, but structurally checked</p> <p>This is the strongest public trust level.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#53-rules","title":"5.3 Rules","text":"<ul> <li>Systems MUST NOT emit <code>verified</code> without actual integrity basis.</li> <li>Systems SHOULD NOT emit <code>traceable</code> if no meaningful lineage can be traversed.</li> <li>Systems MAY downgrade a result\u2019s evidence level under degraded mode.</li> <li><code>intent=\"audit\"</code> SHOULD preferentially return <code>traceable</code> or <code>verified</code> items where possible.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#6-trust-grades-optional-aggregate-view","title":"6. Trust Grades (Optional Aggregate View)","text":"<p>Some consumers may want a higher-level summary beyond evidence levels.</p> <p>A runtime MAY expose an aggregate trust grade:</p> <pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass TrustGrade(TypedDict):\n    grade: Literal[\"T0\", \"T1\", \"T2\", \"T3\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre> <p>Suggested interpretation - T0 \u2014 low trust / informational only - T1 \u2014 provenance known - T2 \u2014 causally traceable - T3 \u2014 integrity-backed / verified</p> <p>This is optional and MUST NOT replace the underlying explicit signals.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#7-degraded-mode-semantics","title":"7. Degraded Mode Semantics","text":"<p>Degraded mode is a first-class trust signal, not a hidden implementation detail.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#71-definition","title":"7.1 Definition","text":"<p>A system is in degraded mode when one or more unavailable, stale, or impaired components materially affects: - retrieval quality - verification power - policy enforcement - coordination confidence - export fidelity</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#72-examples","title":"7.2 Examples","text":"<p>The following SHOULD trigger visible degradation signals when materially relevant: - embedder unavailable - vector retrieval disabled - graph enrichment unavailable - ledger verification stale or pending - taint propagation unavailable - event bus unavailable where required for coordination visibility - agent liveness unknown - export adapter unavailable - memory portability/export unavailable when explicitly requested</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#73-visibility-requirements","title":"7.3 Visibility requirements","text":"<p>Degraded mode SHOULD become visible through one or more of: - <code>HealthReport.status = \"degraded\"</code> - <code>HealthReport.degraded_features</code> - <code>AcceptanceResult.warnings</code> - <code>QueryPlan.degraded_mode = true</code> - <code>QueryPlan.fallback_reason</code> - lowered <code>QueryEvidenceLevel</code> - <code>FailureResult</code> when a forced strategy or capability cannot be honored</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#74-required-behavior-under-degraded-mode","title":"7.4 Required behavior under degraded mode","text":"<p>For strategy=\"auto\" The system MAY fallback to a weaker route, but MUST expose: - chosen strategy - fallback reason - degraded mode</p> <p>For forced strategy requests If the requested route cannot be honored: - return <code>FailureResult</code>, or - only fallback if explicit contract allows it</p> <p>Silent substitution is forbidden.</p> <p>For intent=\"audit\" The system SHOULD be more conservative: - downgrade confidence aggressively when evidence weakens - prefer fewer trustworthy results over many weak ones - surface provenance gaps - avoid presenting degraded results as equivalent to healthy-path results</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#8-rejection-vs-failure-semantics","title":"8. Rejection vs Failure Semantics","text":"<p>This distinction is non-negotiable.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#81-rejection","title":"8.1 Rejection","text":"<p>A rejection means:</p> <p>the system intentionally refused an operation according to rule, policy, trust posture, or governance logic.</p> <p>It is not a crash. It is not a missing dependency. It is an act of enforcement.</p> <p>Canonical shape:</p> <pre><code>class RejectionResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"rejection\"]\n    category: Literal[\"policy\", \"safety\", \"consistency\", \"integrity\", \"compliance\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    layer: Literal[\"guard\", \"membrane\", \"policy\", \"verification\"]\n    rule_id: str\n    severity: Literal[\"low\", \"medium\", \"high\", \"critical\"]\n    evidence: list\n    remediation: list[str]\n    metadata: dict\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#82-failure","title":"8.2 Failure","text":"<p>A failure means:</p> <p>the system could not perform the requested operation due to runtime or dependency conditions.</p> <p>Canonical shape:</p> <pre><code>class FailureResult(TypedDict):\n    ok: Literal[False]\n    kind: Literal[\"failure\"]\n    category: Literal[\"dependency\", \"storage\", \"runtime\", \"timeout\", \"capability\"]\n    code: str\n    message: str\n    retryable: bool\n    component: str\n    metadata: dict\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#83-hard-rules","title":"8.3 Hard rules","text":"<ul> <li>Dependency outages MUST NOT be represented as rejections.</li> <li>Policy blocks MUST NOT be represented as failures.</li> <li>Consumers MUST be able to distinguish governance posture from runtime instability.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#84-examples","title":"8.4 Examples","text":"<p>Rejection - contradiction detected - taint threshold exceeded - policy blocked scope - deprecated write path blocked - quorum denied under governance rules</p> <p>Failure - embedder offline - database lock timeout - ledger I/O failure - capability disabled at runtime - event dispatch failure - verification scope invalid due to malformed input</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#9-integrity-semantics","title":"9. Integrity Semantics","text":"<p>Integrity is the public representation of structural validity around stored or inferred state.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#91-integrity-does-not-mean-truth","title":"9.1 Integrity does not mean truth","text":"<p>Integrity means: - chain structure is consistent - cryptographic or structural checks passed - the record has not obviously drifted from its expected lineage or checkpoint basis</p> <p>It does NOT mean: - the content is philosophically true - the source is omniscient - the model never hallucinated upstream</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#92-public-integrity-states","title":"9.2 Public integrity states","text":"<pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass IntegrityState(TypedDict):\n    status: Literal[\"unknown\", \"partial\", \"verified\", \"failed\", \"stale\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#93-semantics","title":"9.3 Semantics","text":"<ul> <li>unknown \u2014 no integrity evaluation available</li> <li>partial \u2014 only subset or limited path checked</li> <li>verified \u2014 requested or linked integrity checks passed</li> <li>failed \u2014 structural check failed</li> <li>stale \u2014 integrity state exists but is too old or insufficiently current for strong claims</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#94-verification-scopes","title":"9.4 Verification scopes","text":"<p>Public verification SHOULD distinguish scope: - fact - project - tenant - time range - tx chain</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#95-verify-result-expectations","title":"9.5 Verify result expectations","text":"<p>A <code>VerifyResult</code> SHOULD report: - requested scope - checked item count - failed item count - integrity state - legacy path involvement - full vs partial verification</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#96-legacy-path-warning","title":"9.6 Legacy path warning","text":"<p>If integrity verification depends on legacy hash or schema paths, that fact SHOULD be surfaced in: - warnings - metadata - verify result notes</p> <p>Because \u201cverified\u201d by archaeological compatibility is not the same as \u201cverified\u201d on clean modern rails.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#10-taint-semantics","title":"10. Taint Semantics","text":"<p>Taint models downstream trust contamination or risk inheritance.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#101-definition","title":"10.1 Definition","text":"<p>Taint is a trust degradation signal that propagates through dependency or causality structures when upstream inputs, decisions, or facts are suspect, contradictory, compromised, or insufficiently trustworthy.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#102-taint-is-not-binary","title":"10.2 Taint is not binary","text":"<p>Public surfaces SHOULD expose at least a small number of taint states.</p> <pre><code>from typing import Literal, TypedDict\n\n\nclass TaintState(TypedDict):\n    status: Literal[\"none\", \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\", \"unknown\"]\n    reason: str\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#103-suggested-interpretation","title":"10.3 Suggested interpretation","text":"<ul> <li>none \u2014 no active taint detected</li> <li>low \u2014 weak taint signals or low-confidence contamination path</li> <li>medium \u2014 meaningful contamination risk</li> <li>high \u2014 serious contamination risk; downstream trust strongly reduced</li> <li>unknown \u2014 taint system unavailable or insufficiently evaluated</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#104-public-behavior","title":"10.4 Public behavior","text":"<ul> <li>high taint SHOULD strongly affect confidence, evidence level, or rejection posture</li> <li>unknown taint MUST NOT be silently treated as none</li> <li><code>intent=\"audit\"</code> SHOULD surface taint explicitly when requested or relevant</li> </ul>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#105-open-vs-premium-guidance","title":"10.5 Open vs premium guidance","text":"<ul> <li>Open tier MAY expose current taint visibility</li> <li>Premium tier MAY expose persistent taint propagation, policy thresholds, and exportable taint lineage</li> </ul> <p>This split is acceptable only if open tier still exposes enough truth not to mislead consumers.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#11-query-trust-semantics","title":"11. Query Trust Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#111-query-is-not-just-retrieval","title":"11.1 Query is not just retrieval","text":"<p>A query result is a trust-bearing object containing: - content - confidence - provenance posture - routing plan - degradation context - optional taint/trace posture</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#112-required-semantics-for-queryresult","title":"11.2 Required semantics for QueryResult","text":"<p>If strategy=\"auto\": - <code>plan.selected_strategy</code> MUST be present</p> <p>If fallback materially changes route: - <code>plan.fallback_used = true</code> - <code>plan.fallback_reason</code> SHOULD be present</p> <p>If runtime degradation materially affects the result: - <code>plan.degraded_mode = true</code></p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#113-intent-specific-expectations","title":"11.3 Intent-specific expectations","text":"<p>lookup Optimize for concrete retrieval with reasonable trust visibility.</p> <p>explore Optimize for breadth, relation, and expansion, but still expose degradation and provenance limits.</p> <p>audit Optimize for trust clarity: - prefer fewer, stronger results - expose evidence level clearly - expose provenance gaps - degrade conservatively - avoid overconfident ranking of weakly sourced items</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#114-confidence-vs-trust","title":"11.4 Confidence vs trust","text":"<p>Confidence score is not equivalent to trust.</p> <p>A result may have: - high lexical or vector score - low evidence level - unknown integrity - medium taint - degraded routing path</p> <p>Consumers MUST be able to distinguish \u201cmatched strongly\u201d from \u201ctrusted strongly.\u201d</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#12-write-path-trust-semantics","title":"12. Write Path Trust Semantics","text":""},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#121-a-successful-write-is-not-just-storage","title":"12.1 A successful write is not just storage","text":"<p>A successful write means the system: - accepted the operation under current trust policy - completed storage path - returned any required warnings - attached operation metadata where applicable</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#122-a-rejected-write-is-governance-not-breakage","title":"12.2 A rejected write is governance, not breakage","text":"<p>Examples: - contradiction with canonical fact - taint threshold exceeded - compliance scope violation - policy forbids raw write into protected tenant - deprecated path blocked</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#123-a-write-under-degraded-mode","title":"12.3 A write under degraded mode","text":"<p>If the write succeeds under degraded conditions, the system SHOULD warn when the degradation is materially relevant.</p> <p>Examples: - accepted without vector enrichment - accepted while compliance export unavailable - accepted while downstream event dispatch degraded</p> <p>Do not pretend the path was pristine if it was limping.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#13-coordination-trust-semantics","title":"13. Coordination Trust Semantics","text":"<p>Coordination is the noisiest place to hallucinate certainty. Do not.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#131-consensus-is-not-omniscience","title":"13.1 Consensus is not omniscience","text":"<p>Public consensus results should reflect: - participating agents - available liveness posture - confidence/weighting basis where exposed - degraded conditions - quorum state</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#132-required-caution-zones","title":"13.2 Required caution zones","text":"<p>The following conditions SHOULD reduce coordination trust or trigger warnings: - stale agents - liveness unknown - degraded event visibility - mixed legacy vote paths - insufficient quorum - disabled consensus capability - incomplete tracking path</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#133-coordination-truth-posture","title":"13.3 Coordination truth posture","text":"<p>Do not present a coordination result as fully trustworthy when: - liveness is missing - tracking is partial - vote schema is in deprecation transition - event bus evidence is missing where expected</p> <p>A swarm with no pulse is just a rumor.</p>"},{"location":"TRUST-SEMANTICS/#14-runtime-truth-semantics","title":"14. Multi-Tenant Isolation Hierarchy","text":"<p>v6 introduces strict cryptographic and logical isolation. Data belonging to a <code>tenant_id</code> is isolated at three levels:</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l3-isolation-ledger","title":"L3 Isolation (Ledger)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: PostgreSQL / AlloyDB with Row-Level Security (RLS).</li> <li>Enforcement: Each database session is scoped to a specific <code>tenant_id</code>.</li> <li>Query: <code>SELECT * FROM facts WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('cortex.tenant_id')</code></li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l2-isolation-vector-search","title":"L2 Isolation (Vector Search)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: Qdrant Cloud / Distributed Clusters.</li> <li>Enforcement: Payload filtering in every semantic search.</li> <li>Constraint: <code>FieldCondition(key=\"tenant_id\", match=MatchValue(value=tenant_id))</code> is automatically injected by <code>VectorStoreL2.recall()</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#l1-isolation-working-memory","title":"L1 Isolation (Working Memory)","text":"<ul> <li>Engine: Redis / Local LRU.</li> <li>Enforcement: Cache keys prefixed with <code>tenant_id:session_id</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#2-distributed-rbac-engine","title":"2. Distributed RBAC Engine","text":"<p>CORTEX v6 moves beyond simple API keys to a granular permission system.</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#role-definitions","title":"Role Definitions","text":"Role Scope Permissions <code>SOVEREIGN_ROOT</code> Global Can manage all tenants, billing, and infra. <code>TENANT_ADMIN</code> Tenant Can manage keys, projects, and users within a tenant. <code>AGENT_EXECUTOR</code> Project Can <code>read</code>, <code>write</code>, and <code>vote</code> on facts within specific projects. <code>AUDITOR</code> Tenant/Project Read-only access to transaction logs and Merkle checkpoints."},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#permission-scopes","title":"Permission Scopes","text":"<ul> <li><code>memory:read</code>: Access to semantic recall.</li> <li><code>memory:write</code>: Access to <code>process_interaction</code>.</li> <li><code>ledger:verify</code>: Access to integrity checks.</li> <li><code>consensus:vote</code>: Ability to influence <code>consensus_score</code>.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#3-data-flow-distributed","title":"3. Data Flow (Distributed)","text":"<p>```mermaid sequenceDiagram     participant App as External App     participant GW as CORTEX Gateway (GraphQL)     participant RBAC as RBAC Engine     participant Cache as Redis (L1)     participant DB as AlloyDB (L3)     participant Vec as Qdrant (L2)</p> <pre><code>App-&gt;&gt;GW: Query context (JWT + Query)\nGW-&gt;&gt;RBAC: Validate Session (tenant_id, scope)\nRBAC--&gt;&gt;GW: OK (tenant-A)\nGW-&gt;&gt;Cache: Fetch recent window (tenant-A:session-X)\nCache--&gt;&gt;GW: L1 Context\nGW-&gt;&gt;Vec: Semantic search (tenant_id=\"tenant-A\")\nVec--&gt;&gt;GW: L2 Context (Isolated)\nGW-&gt;&gt;DB: Audit Log\nGW--&gt;&gt;App: Unified Context\n</code></pre> <p>```</p>"},{"location":"V6_ARCHITECTURE/#4-migration-strategy-v5-v6","title":"4. Solo la verificaci\u00f3n puede gobernar.</p>"},{"location":"architecture/#system-overview","title":"System Overview","text":"<p>CORTEX is a trust infrastructure engine that provides cryptographic verification, immutable audit trails, and regulatory compliance for AI agent memory. It is built under the strict premise that generative AI output is fundamentally thermodynamically unstable conjecture (<code>Void-State</code>). It only becomes durable state after surviving a predefined path of deterministic filters.</p> <p>To enforce this, it combines a relational database with vector embeddings, hash-chained transactions, Merkle tree integrity, multi-agent consensus, and privacy protection \u2014 running locally on SQLite or scaling to AlloyDB + Qdrant + Redis for enterprise deployments.</p> <p>```mermaid graph TB     subgraph Interfaces         CLI[\"CLI38 commands\"]         API[\"REST APIFastAPI\"]         MCP[\"MCP ServerModel Context Protocol\"]         GraphQL[\"GraphQL(coming Q2)\"]     end</p> <pre><code>subgraph Gateway[\"Trust Gateway\"]\n    Auth[\"Auth Manager&lt;br/&gt;HMAC-SHA256 + RBAC\"]\n    Privacy[\"Privacy Shield&lt;br/&gt;11 secret patterns\"]\n    RateLimit[\"Rate Limiter&lt;br/&gt;Sliding window\"]\n    Security[\"Security Headers&lt;br/&gt;CSP, HSTS\"]\nend\n\nsubgraph Engine[\"Core Engine\"]\n    CortexEngine[\"CortexEngine&lt;br/&gt;(Composite Orchestrator)\"]\n    AsyncEngine[\"AsyncCortexEngine&lt;br/&gt;(Native Async + Pool)\"]\n    FactManager[\"FactManager\"]\n    EmbeddingManager[\"EmbeddingManager\"]\n    ConsensusManager[\"ConsensusManager\"]\nend\n\nsubgraph Memory[\"Tripartite Memory\"]\n    L1[\"L1: Working Memory&lt;br/&gt;Redis / In-Memory\"]\n    L2[\"L2: Vector Memory&lt;br/&gt;sqlite-vec / Qdrant\"]\n    L3[\"L3: Episodic Ledger&lt;br/&gt;SQLite / AlloyDB\"]\nend\n\nsubgraph Trust[\"Trust Layer\"]\n    Ledger[\"SHA-256 Hash Chain\"]\n    Merkle[\"Merkle Tree Checkpoints\"]\n    Consensus[\"WBFT Consensus\"]\n    Sandbox[\"AST Sandbox\"]\nend\n\nsubgraph Services[\"Platform Services\"]\n    Daemon[\"Self-Healing Daemon&lt;br/&gt;13 monitors\"]\n    Compactor[\"Compaction Sidecar\"]\n    Sync[\"Sync Engine\"]\n    Notifications[\"Notification Bus\"]\n    Timing[\"Time Tracker\"]\nend\n\nCLI --&gt; Facts are immutable records with temporal validity:</p> Field Type Description <code>id</code> INTEGER Auto-incremented primary key <code>project</code> TEXT Namespace (tenant isolation) <code>content</code> TEXT The information itself <code>fact_type</code> TEXT <code>knowledge</code>, <code>decision</code>, <code>error</code>, <code>ghost</code>, <code>config</code>, <code>bridge</code>, <code>axiom</code>, <code>rule</code> <code>tags</code> JSON Searchable labels <code>confidence</code> TEXT <code>stated</code>, <code>inferred</code>, <code>observed</code>, <code>verified</code>, <code>disputed</code> <code>valid_from</code> DATETIME When the fact became true <code>valid_until</code> DATETIME When deprecated (NULL = active) <code>source</code> TEXT Origin agent or process (auto-detected) <code>meta</code> JSON Arbitrary metadata <code>consensus_score</code> REAL Weighted agreement (default 1.0) <code>tx_id</code> INTEGER FK to creating transaction <code>tenant_id</code> TEXT Multi-tenant scope"},{"location":"architecture/#temporal-queries","title":"Temporal Queries","text":"<p>Every fact has a temporal window (<code>valid_from</code> \u2192 <code>valid_until</code>):</p> <ul> <li>Current view: <code>WHERE valid_until IS NULL</code></li> <li>Point-in-time: <code>WHERE valid_from &lt;= ? AND (valid_until IS NULL OR valid_until &gt; ?)</code></li> <li>Time travel: Reconstruct database state at any transaction ID</li> <li>History: Full timeline including deprecated facts</li> </ul>"},{"location":"architecture/#hash-chained-ledger","title":"Hash-Chained Ledger","text":"<p>Every mutation creates a transaction with a SHA-256 hash linked to the previous one:</p> <pre><code>TX #1: hash = SHA256(\"GENESIS\" + project + action + detail + timestamp)\nTX #2: hash = SHA256(hash_1 + project + action + detail + timestamp)\nTX #N: hash = SHA256(hash_{N-1} + ...)\n</code></pre> <p>This creates a tamper-evident audit trail. <code>verify_ledger()</code> walks the chain and reports any breaks.</p>"},{"location":"architecture/#merkle-tree-checkpoints","title":"Merkle Tree Checkpoints","text":"<p>Periodically, the ledger creates Merkle tree checkpoints from batches of fact hashes. These enable: - O(log N) integrity verification - Efficient synchronization between nodes - Batch proof generation for compliance audits</p>"},{"location":"architecture/#multi-agent-consensus-wbft","title":"Multi-Agent Consensus (WBFT)","text":"<p>CORTEX implements Weighted Byzantine Fault Tolerance:</p> <ol> <li>Tracks reputation scores per agent (0.0\u20131.0) with decay</li> <li>Weighs votes by agent reputation</li> <li>Domain-specific vote multipliers</li> <li>Updates <code>consensus_score</code> on each fact</li> <li>Elder Council verdict for edge cases without quorum</li> <li>Immutable vote ledger for audit</li> </ol>"},{"location":"architecture/#engine-evolution-the-omega-manifold","title":"Engine Evolution: The Omega Manifold (\u03a9)","text":"<p>En la transici\u00f3n hacia las v7 y v8, CORTEX introduce el Manifold Omega, una capa de orquestaci\u00f3n 4D que unifica percepci\u00f3n, decisi\u00f3n y acci\u00f3n.</p> <p>Los pilares fundamentales: - KETER-\u03a9: Meta-orquestaci\u00f3n soberana. Cognitiva","text":"<p>Optimizaci\u00f3n, Persistencia y Verificabilidad en Agentes de IA de Pr\u00f3xima Generaci\u00f3n</p> <p>[!NOTE] Este documento establece el marco te\u00f3rico-pr\u00e1ctico que sustenta la arquitectura de CORTEX y el Enjambre Centauro. Cada secci\u00f3n mapea directamente a componentes implementados o en roadmap.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#introduccion-el-imperativo-de-la-evolucion-arquitectonica","title":"Introducci\u00f3n: El Imperativo de la Evoluci\u00f3n Arquitect\u00f3nica","text":"<p>La trayectoria actual de la Inteligencia Artificial generativa ha alcanzado un punto de inflexi\u00f3n cr\u00edtico. Los sistemas de primera generaci\u00f3n, caracterizados por interacciones ap\u00e1tridas y recuperaciones de informaci\u00f3n simplistas, se enfrentan a un \"techo de inteligencia\" que limita su aplicabilidad en entornos empresariales y cr\u00edticos.</p> <p>La pregunta fundamental de \"c\u00f3mo mejorarlo\" no se responde simplemente incrementando el tama\u00f1o de los par\u00e1metros de los modelos, sino mediante una reingenier\u00eda profunda de la arquitectura subyacente que soporta la cognici\u00f3n sint\u00e9tica.</p> <p>Para transformar asistentes reactivos en agentes soberanos capaces de razonamiento estrat\u00e9gico y ejecuci\u00f3n aut\u00f3noma, es necesario abordar cuatro pilares de optimizaci\u00f3n:</p> <ol> <li>La eficiencia en la recuperaci\u00f3n de datos a hiperescala</li> <li>La estructuraci\u00f3n de la memoria a largo plazo</li> <li>La implementaci\u00f3n de bucles de razonamiento metacognitivo</li> <li>La instauraci\u00f3n de garant\u00edas de seguridad criptogr\u00e1fica</li> </ol> <p>El estado actual del arte, dominado por la Generaci\u00f3n Aumentada por Recuperaci\u00f3n (RAG) convencional, sufre de amnesia contextual y latencia operativa. Los agentes carecen de una comprensi\u00f3n persistente del mundo y operan bajo un paradigma de confianza ciega que es inaceptable para sectores regulados.</p> <p>La mejora sustancial de estos sistemas requiere la transici\u00f3n hacia arquitecturas de memoria unificada que integren vectores, gr\u00e1ficos de conocimiento y series temporales, respaldadas por mecanismos de consenso bizantino y pruebas de conocimiento cero. Este informe detalla la hoja de ruta t\u00e9cnica para elevar la IA desde la automatizaci\u00f3n de tareas (Nivel 2) hacia la colaboraci\u00f3n estrat\u00e9gica aut\u00f3noma (Nivel 4), estableciendo nuevos est\u00e1ndares en rendimiento computacional y soberan\u00eda de datos.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#1-el-sustrato-de-la-velocidad-optimizacion-vectorial-y-cuantizacion-en-el-borde","title":"1. El Sustrato de la Velocidad: Optimizaci\u00f3n Vectorial y Cuantizaci\u00f3n en el Borde","text":"<p>La capacidad de un agente para recuperar informaci\u00f3n relevante en tiempo real es el cuello de botella primario en la experiencia de usuario y la eficacia operativa. Las implementaciones iniciales, que dependen de b\u00fasquedas vectoriales de fuerza bruta, se vuelven insostenibles a medida que los corpus de conocimiento escalan hacia los miles de millones de vectores.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#11-de-la-busqueda-lineal-a-la-navegacion-jerarquica-hnsw","title":"1.1 De la B\u00fasqueda Lineal a la Navegaci\u00f3n Jer\u00e1rquica (HNSW)","text":"<p>En los sistemas de recuperaci\u00f3n b\u00e1sicos, la identificaci\u00f3n de los vecinos m\u00e1s cercanos (K-Nearest Neighbors, KNN) se realiza mediante un escaneo exhaustivo que calcula la distancia \u2014 ya sea Euclidiana o de Coseno \u2014 entre el vector de consulta y cada uno de los vectores almacenados en la base de datos.</p> <p>Este enfoque presenta una complejidad computacional de O(N \u00b7 D), donde N representa el n\u00famero total de vectores y D la dimensionalidad del embedding. Cuando se opera con modelos modernos como <code>text-embedding-3-small</code> de OpenAI (1536 dimensiones) sobre un conjunto de un mill\u00f3n de registros, el sistema debe ejecutar miles de millones de operaciones de punto flotante por cada consulta individual, resultando en latencias inaceptables para aplicaciones interactivas.</p> <p>La soluci\u00f3n reside en la implementaci\u00f3n de \u00edndices de Mundo Peque\u00f1o Jer\u00e1rquico Navegable (HNSW):</p> <p><code>mermaid graph TB     subgraph \"Capa 3 \u2014 Autopista Global\"         A((N1)) -.-&gt; B((N47))         B -.-&gt; C((N215))     end     subgraph \"Capa 2 \u2014 Conexiones Intermedias\"         D((N1)) --&gt; E((N12)) --&gt; F((N28))         F --&gt; G((N47))     end     subgraph \"Capa 1 \u2014 Red Densa Local\"         H((N1)) --&gt; I((N3)) --&gt; J((N5))         J --&gt; K((N8)) --&gt; L((N12))     end     A --&gt; D     D --&gt; H     B --&gt; G     G --&gt; L     style A fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560     style B fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560     style C fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560</code></p> <p>HNSW reestructura el espacio vectorial como un grafo multicapa. En las capas superiores, los nodos est\u00e1n conectados de manera escasa, permitiendo \"saltos\" largos a trav\u00e9s del espacio sem\u00e1ntico. A medida que la b\u00fasqueda desciende a capas inferiores, la densidad aumenta, permitiendo refinamiento local. Esto transforma la complejidad a O(log N).</p> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El m\u00f3dulo <code>sqlite-vec</code> integrado en CORTEX utiliza HNSW como \u00edndice primario para la tabla <code>vec_facts</code>. La configuraci\u00f3n actual contempla <code>M=16</code> conexiones por nodo y <code>efConstruction=200</code> para balancear recall vs. velocidad de indexaci\u00f3n.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#12-cuantizacion-binaria-compresion-radical-y-aceleracion-por-hardware","title":"1.2 Cuantizaci\u00f3n Binaria: Compresi\u00f3n Radical y Aceleraci\u00f3n por Hardware","text":"<p>La Cuantizaci\u00f3n Binaria (BQ) comprime cada dimensi\u00f3n del vector a un solo bit: los valores positivos se convierten en <code>1</code> y los negativos o cero en <code>0</code>.</p> M\u00e9trica float32 Binario Factor Almacenamiento por vector (384d) 1,536 bytes 48 bytes 32\u00d7 \u00cdndice 1M vectores ~1.5 GB ~48 MB 32\u00d7 Operaci\u00f3n de similitud Producto punto FP XOR + <code>popcount</code> 15-45\u00d7 m\u00e1s r\u00e1pido Recall@10 100% ~92-95% Marginal <p>Al operar con vectores binarios, el c\u00e1lculo de similitud utiliza la Distancia de Hamming \u2014 operaciones l\u00f3gicas XOR seguidas de conteo de bits activados (<code>popcount</code>), instrucciones optimizadas a nivel de hardware en CPUs modernas (AVX-512 en x86, NEON en ARM).</p> <p>[!TIP] Estrategia de re-puntuaci\u00f3n (rescoring): Usar el \u00edndice binario para recuperar un conjunto candidato amplio (top-100 ANN) y aplicar los vectores originales float32 solo a estos candidatos para el ranking final. Velocidad binaria + precisi\u00f3n float32.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#13-arquitecturas-de-almacenamiento-shadow-tables-y-diskann","title":"1.3 Arquitecturas de Almacenamiento: Shadow Tables y DiskANN","text":"<p>La integraci\u00f3n de capacidades vectoriales en bases de datos relacionales utiliza tablas sombra (shadow tables). Las implementaciones como <code>sqlite-vec</code> serializan los nodos del grafo HNSW directamente en tablas internas del motor de base de datos, asegurando que las operaciones vectoriales cumplan las propiedades ACID.</p> <p>Para escenarios hiperescala donde el \u00edndice excede la RAM \u2014 situaci\u00f3n com\u00fan en agentes soberanos sobre hardware modesto \u2014 la arquitectura \u00f3ptima es DiskANN:</p> <ul> <li>Dise\u00f1ado para aprovechar SSDs modernos</li> <li>Optimiza el grafo para minimizar lecturas aleatorias de disco</li> <li>Sirve consultas con \u00edndices 10\u00d7 mayores que la RAM del sistema</li> <li>Rompe la dependencia de hardware costoso</li> </ul> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El <code>EmbeddingPrunerMixin</code> implementado en <code>cortex/engine/embeddings.py</code> gestiona la compresi\u00f3n adaptativa. Cuando se alcanza el umbral de embeddings (<code>CORTEX_MAX_EMBEDDINGS</code>), el pruner aplica cuantizaci\u00f3n escalar y eliminaci\u00f3n de vectores de baja utilidad.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#2-ingenieria-de-contexto-y-memoria-persistente-mas-alla-del-rag-estatico","title":"2. Ingenier\u00eda de Contexto y Memoria Persistente: M\u00e1s All\u00e1 del RAG Est\u00e1tico","text":"<p>La mejora cualitativa trasciende la velocidad de recuperaci\u00f3n \u2014 reside en la calidad y estructuraci\u00f3n del contexto. El paradigma RAG tradicional muestra deficiencias cr\u00edticas en razonamiento global, comprensi\u00f3n de relaciones indirectas y memoria a largo plazo.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#21-graphrag-y-la-dimension-temporal","title":"2.1 GraphRAG y la Dimensi\u00f3n Temporal","text":"<p>El RAG est\u00e1ndar opera bajo una limitaci\u00f3n estructural: recupera \"puntos\" de datos aislados sin comprender la topolog\u00eda que los une.</p> <p>GraphRAG aborda esta deficiencia mediante:</p> <ol> <li>Extracci\u00f3n de entidades y relaciones del texto crudo usando LLMs</li> <li>Detecci\u00f3n de comunidades (algoritmo de Leiden) para identificar cl\u00fasteres tem\u00e1ticos</li> <li>Res\u00famenes jer\u00e1rquicos pre-computados para consultas de alto nivel</li> <li>Aristas temporales (framework Graphiti) que distinguen hechos vigentes vs. obsoletos</li> </ol> <p><code>mermaid graph LR     A[\"Documento crudo\"] --&gt; B[\"Extracci\u00f3n de entidades\"]     B --&gt; C[\"Grafo de Conocimiento\"]     C --&gt; D[\"Detecci\u00f3n de comunidades\"]     D --&gt; E[\"Res\u00famenes jer\u00e1rquicos\"]     C --&gt; F[\"Aristas temporales\"]     F --&gt; G[\"Invalidaci\u00f3n autom\u00e1tica\"]     E --&gt; H[\"Consulta global\"]     G --&gt; H     style A fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560     style H fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a</code></p> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El m\u00f3dulo <code>cortex/engine/graph/</code> implementa un grafo de conocimiento con aristas temporales via <code>valid_from</code>/<code>valid_until</code> en cada fact. El <code>QueryMixin.search()</code> combina b\u00fasqueda vectorial + filtrado temporal via <code>as_of</code>.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#22-arquitectura-unificada-de-memoria-el-modelo-postgresql","title":"2.2 Arquitectura Unificada de Memoria: El Modelo PostgreSQL","text":"<p>La fragmentaci\u00f3n de la infraestructura introduce latencia y complejidad que obstaculizan la autonom\u00eda del agente. La arquitectura de referencia unifica tres tipos de memoria cognitiva:</p> Tipo de Memoria Funci\u00f3n Cognitiva Implementaci\u00f3n en CORTEX Epis\u00f3dica Registro autobiogr\u00e1fico secuencial (\"\u00bfQu\u00e9 tarea realizamos ayer?\") Tabla <code>facts</code> con <code>created_at</code> como series temporales. Consultas de rango temporal via <code>time_travel()</code> Sem\u00e1ntica Conocimiento cristalizado y hechos recuperables <code>vec_facts</code> con embeddings <code>all-MiniLM-L6-v2</code> (384d) + HNSW index Procedimental Habilidades, preferencias, reglas de negocio Tabla <code>facts</code> con <code>fact_type='procedure'</code> + <code>confidence='verified'</code> <p>Esta unificaci\u00f3n permite consultas cognitivas complejas en una sola transacci\u00f3n SQL:</p> <pre><code>-- Ejemplo: Recuperar las 5 \u00faltimas interacciones sobre \"seguridad\" con confianza verificada\nSELECT f.content, f.created_at, f.confidence\nFROM facts f\nJOIN vec_facts v ON v.fact_id = f.id\nWHERE f.project = 'moskv-swarm'\n  AND f.created_at &gt; datetime('now', '-7 days')   -- Memoria epis\u00f3dica\n  AND f.confidence = 'verified'                     -- Memoria procedimental\nORDER BY vec_distance_cosine(v.embedding, ?)        -- Memoria sem\u00e1ntica\nLIMIT 5;\n</code></pre> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El pipeline verificado en el smoke test (<code>smoke_test_cortex_pipeline.py</code>) demostr\u00f3 la unificaci\u00f3n: <code>post_fact</code> (sem\u00e1ntica), <code>persist_mission_insight</code> (procedimental) y <code>get_summary</code> (epis\u00f3dica) operan sobre la misma base SQLite con integridad ACID.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#23-busqueda-hibrida-y-fusion-de-rangos-rrf","title":"2.3 B\u00fasqueda H\u00edbrida y Fusi\u00f3n de Rangos (RRF)","text":"<p>Los modelos de embedding sobresalen en matices sem\u00e1nticos (\"veh\u00edculo\" \u2194 \"coche\") pero fallan en coincidencia exacta de palabras clave, c\u00f3digos o nombres propios.</p> <p>La Fusi\u00f3n de Rango Rec\u00edproco (RRF) sintetiza b\u00fasqueda vectorial + l\u00e9xica (BM25):</p> <pre><code>RRF_score(d) = \u03a3 1 / (k + rank_i(d))\n</code></pre> <p>Donde <code>k</code> es una constante de suavizado (t\u00edpicamente 60) y <code>rank_i(d)</code> es la posici\u00f3n del documento <code>d</code> en la lista de resultados del m\u00e9todo <code>i</code>.</p> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El <code>SearchMixin</code> en <code>cortex/engine/search.py</code> implementa b\u00fasqueda h\u00edbrida combinando <code>sqlite-vec</code> (vectorial) + FTS5 (l\u00e9xica) con RRF. El <code>recall_context()</code> del bridge actual usa fallback de texto (LIKE queries) cuando <code>sqlite-vec</code> no est\u00e1 disponible.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#3-modelos-de-mundo-y-razonamiento-estrategico-el-sistema-2-de-la-ia","title":"3. Modelos de Mundo y Razonamiento Estrat\u00e9gico: El Sistema 2 de la IA","text":"<p>Una vez resueltos los desaf\u00edos de almacenamiento y memoria, la mejora cualitativa del agente depende de su capacidad de razonamiento. Los agentes actuales operan en modo reactivo (Sistema 1). Para tareas complejas, es imperativo implementar el Sistema 2: deliberativo, planificador y autocr\u00edtico.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#31-arquitectura-aero-y-el-doble-bucle-de-aprendizaje","title":"3.1 Arquitectura AERO y el Doble Bucle de Aprendizaje","text":"<p>El framework AERO (Autonomous Evolutionary Reasoning Optimization) implementa un sistema de doble bucle end\u00f3geno:</p> <p><code>mermaid graph TD     subgraph \"Bucle Interno \u2014 F\u00e1brica de Datos\"         G[\"\ud83c\udfaf Generador&lt;br/&gt;(Self-Questioning)\"] --&gt; S[\"\ud83d\udd27 Solucionador&lt;br/&gt;(Self-Answering)\"]         S --&gt; C[\"\ud83d\udd0d Cr\u00edtico&lt;br/&gt;(Self-Criticism)\"]         C --&gt;|\"Rechaza\"| G         C --&gt;|\"Aprueba\"| V[\"\u2705 Experiencia Validada\"]     end     subgraph \"Bucle Externo \u2014 Consolidaci\u00f3n\"         V --&gt; F[\"Fine-tuning / Prompt Optimization\"]         F --&gt; M[\"Memoria Procedimental Mejorada\"]         M -.-&gt;|\"ZDP calibrada\"| G     end     style G fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560     style C fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#ffd700     style V fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#16c79a</code></p> <p>Zona de Desarrollo Pr\u00f3ximo (ZDP): Los desaf\u00edos se calibran para estar ligeramente por encima de la competencia actual confirmada del agente \u2014 evitando estancamiento (tareas triviales) y colapso del aprendizaje (tareas imposibles).</p> <p>Correcci\u00f3n Contrafactual Independiente (ICC): El agente verifica conclusiones asumiendo inicialmente que su respuesta es incorrecta e intenta construir un camino l\u00f3gico que justifique el error. Si no lo logra \u2192 confianza reforzada. Si lo logra \u2192 reevaluaci\u00f3n profunda.</p> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a Centauro: El <code>ConsensusBreaker</code> en <code>moskv-swarm/consensus/</code> implementa una versi\u00f3n pr\u00e1ctica del ICC \u2014 cuando los agentes del enjambre no alcanzan consenso, se escala a un <code>ElderCouncil</code> (3 evaluadores independientes) o a <code>HumanEscalation</code> via el <code>SovereignGate</code>.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#32-modelos-de-mundo-para-planificacion-estrategica","title":"3.2 Modelos de Mundo para Planificaci\u00f3n Estrat\u00e9gica","text":"<p>La simulaci\u00f3n mental permite al agente proyectar consecuencias antes de ejecutar:</p> <ul> <li>Antes de <code>delete_file</code> \u2192 simular ruptura de dependencias</li> <li>Antes de <code>deploy_production</code> \u2192 simular impacto en servicios dependientes</li> <li>Antes de <code>execute_trade</code> \u2192 simular impacto en portfolio bajo varios escenarios</li> </ul> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El <code>SovereignGate</code> (<code>cortex/sovereign_gate.py</code>) implementa la barrera de planificaci\u00f3n deliberada \u2014 las acciones L3 (destructivas) requieren aprobaci\u00f3n expl\u00edcita del operador, impidiendo ejecuci\u00f3n reactiva del Sistema 1.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#4-gobernanza-seguridad-y-verificabilidad-la-capa-de-confianza","title":"4. Gobernanza, Seguridad y Verificabilidad: La Capa de Confianza","text":"<p>A medida que los agentes escalan hacia Nivel 4/5 de autonom\u00eda, la seguridad basada en \"guardarra\u00edles de texto\" es insuficiente. La mejora exige garant\u00edas criptogr\u00e1ficas verificables y arquitecturas de confianza cero.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#41-soberania-de-hardware-tees-y-computacion-confidencial","title":"4.1 Soberan\u00eda de Hardware: TEEs y Computaci\u00f3n Confidencial","text":"<p>Los Entornos de Ejecuci\u00f3n Confiables (TEEs) \u2014 Intel TDX, AMD SEV, NVIDIA Confidential Computing \u2014 proporcionan:</p> <ul> <li>Encriptaci\u00f3n en uso: Pesos del modelo y datos del usuario permanecen encriptados en RAM durante toda la inferencia</li> <li>Atestaci\u00f3n remota: El agente puede probar criptogr\u00e1ficamente que ejecuta una versi\u00f3n no adulterada de su c\u00f3digo y modelo</li> </ul> <p>[!CAUTION] Indispensable para el despliegue en finanzas y salud, donde la integridad del algoritmo es tan cr\u00edtica como la confidencialidad de los datos.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#42-auditoria-inmutable-zk-proofs-y-zksql","title":"4.2 Auditor\u00eda Inmutable: ZK-Proofs y ZKSQL","text":"<p>Las Pruebas de Conocimiento Cero (ZK-Proofs) resuelven la \"Paradoja de la Auditor\u00eda\":</p> <pre><code>ZK-Audit Log = Proof(\n    input \u2208 Domain,\n    model = certified_version,\n    filters = [safety_check_1, ..., safety_check_N] ALL PASSED,\n    output = f(input, model, filters)\n) \u2192 VERIFIABLE sin revelar input ni output\n</code></pre> <p>ZKSQL extiende esto a consultas anal\u00edticas \u2014 un regulador puede lanzar queries SQL sobre la base de datos privada y recibir respuestas con prueba criptogr\u00e1fica de correcci\u00f3n y completitud, sin acceso de lectura a registros individuales.</p> <p>[!IMPORTANT] Mapeo a CORTEX: El <code>LedgerMixin</code> (<code>cortex/engine/ledger.py</code>) implementa un log de auditor\u00eda inmutable con hashes can\u00f3nicos SHA-256 encadenados (cada entrada referencia el hash de la anterior). El <code>time_travel()</code> permite reconstrucci\u00f3n forense del estado en cualquier punto temporal.</p>"},{"location":"cognitive_sovereignty_architectures/#43-consenso-en-enjambres-multi-agente","title":"4.3 Consenso en Enjambres Multi-Agente","text":"<p>La integridad del enjambre se \u03b2\u00b7exp(\u03b3\u00b7failures)\n\n# Selecci\u00f3n de l\u00edder via VRF ponderada\nP(agent_i = leader) = T_i / \u03a3 T_j  \u2200j \u2208 swarm\n</code></pre> Evento Impacto en Reputaci\u00f3n Direct OS checks scattered across the codebase are prohibited.</p> <pre><code>from cortex.platform.sys import (\n    is_macos, is_linux, is_windows,\n    platform_name,\n    get_cortex_dir, get_service_dir, get_python_executable,\n    tail_file_command,\n)\n</code></pre> Function Returns <code>is_macos()</code> <code>True</code> on Darwin <code>is_linux()</code> <code>True</code> on Linux <code>is_windows()</code> <code>True</code> on Win32 <code>platform_name()</code> Human-readable: <code>\"macOS\"</code>, <code>\"Linux\"</code>, <code>\"Windows\"</code> <code>get_cortex_dir()</code> <code>~/.cortex</code> (Unix) or <code>%APPDATA%/cortex</code> (Windows) <code>get_service_dir()</code> <code>~/Library/LaunchAgents</code> (macOS), <code>~/.config/systemd/user</code> (Linux), <code>None</code> (Windows) <code>get_python_executable()</code> <code>sys.executable</code> \u2014 no hardcoded paths <code>tail_file_command(path)</code> <code>['tail', '-f', path]</code> (Unix) or <code>['powershell', 'Get-Content', path, '-Wait']</code> (Windows) <p>Dynamic executables: Instead of hardcoding paths like <code>/Users/.../bin/python</code>, the architecture uses <code>get_python_executable()</code> (or <code>sys.executable</code>) to ensure daemons and subshells spawn using the correct Python binary regardless of install location.</p>"},{"location":"cross_platform_guide/#native-notifications","title":"Native Notifications","text":"<p>The Notifier component (<code>cortex/daemon/notifier.py</code>) uses adaptive dispatch for system-native alerts:</p> Platform Method Integration macOS <code>osascript</code> Notification Center Linux <code>notify-send</code> libnotify Windows PowerShell <code>System.Windows.Forms.NotifyIcon</code> (balloon tip) Fallback Logger <code>INFO</code>/<code>WARNING</code> if no UI session"},{"location":"cross_platform_guide/#daemons-service-managers","title":"Daemons &amp; All paths must be relative or derived dynamically:</p> <pre><code># \u2705 Correct\nfrom pathlib import Path\nMODULE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent\nDATA_PATH = MODULE_DIR / \"data\" / \"target.json\"\n\n# \u274c Forbidden\nDATA_PATH = \"./data/target.json\"\n</code></pre> <p>This ensures CORTEX remains deployable on any machine without code changes \u2014 the foundation of a truly sovereign, cross-platform agent infrastructure.</p>"},{"location":"deployment/","title":"Deployment","text":"<p>CORTEX supports multiple deployment models \u2014 from a single <code>pip install</code> to production Kubernetes clusters.</p>"},{"location":"deployment/#local-development","title":"Local (Development)","text":"<p>The simplest deployment.  \u251c\u2500\u2500 routes/                # REST API route handlers (20 modules)\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500\u2500 auth/                  # Authentication &amp; RBAC\n\u2502   \u251c\u2500\u2500 implementations:</p>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#1-cortexengine-composite-orchestrator","title":"1. <code>CortexEngine</code> (Composite Orchestrator)","text":"<p>Located in <code>cortex/engine/__init__.py</code>. The main entry point used by both CLI and API:</p> <ul> <li>Composes <code>FactManager</code>, <code>EmbeddingManager</code>, and <code>ConsensusManager</code></li> <li>Provides both sync and async methods via <code>SyncCompatMixin</code></li> <li>Manages database connections and ledger</li> <li>Delegates CRUD to mixins: <code>StoreMixin</code>, <code>QueryMixin</code>, <code>ConsensusMixin</code></li> </ul> <pre><code>from cortex.engine import CortexEngine\n\nengine = CortexEngine(db_path=\"my.db\", auto_embed=True)\nengine.init_db_sync()\n\n# Store\nfact_id = engine.store_sync(\"project-x\", \"Python is great\", fact_type=\"knowledge\")\n\n# Search\nresults = engine.search_sync(\"programming languages\")\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#2-asynccortexengine-api-engine","title":"2. <code>AsyncCortexEngine</code> (API Engine)","text":"<p>Located in <code>cortex/engine_async.py</code>. Used by FastAPI routes for maximum concurrency:</p> <ul> <li>Takes a <code>CortexConnectionPool</code> for connection management</li> <li>All methods are <code>async</code></li> <li>Handles transaction logging and hash chain maintenance</li> </ul> <pre><code>pool = await CortexConnectionPool.create(db_path, pool_size=5)\nengine = AsyncCortexEngine(pool, db_path)\n\nfact_id = await engine.store(project=\"x\", content=\"Hello\", fact_type=\"knowledge\")\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#adding-a-new-route","title":"Adding a New Route","text":"<ol> <li>Create the route file <code>cortex/routes/my_feature.py</code>:</li> </ol> <pre><code>from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends\nfrom cortex.auth import AuthResult, require_permission\nfrom cortex.api.deps import get_async_engine\n\nrouter = APIRouter(tags=[\"my-feature\"])\n\n@router.get(\"/v1/my-feature\")\nasync def my_endpoint(\n    auth: AuthResult = Depends(require_permission(\"read\")),\n    engine = Depends(get_async_engine),\n):\n    # All queries automatically scoped to auth.tenant_id\n    return {\"status\": \"ok\"}\n</code></pre> <ol> <li>Register in <code>cortex/api/core.py</code>:</li> </ol> <pre><code>from cortex.routes.my_feature import router as my_feature_router\napp.include_router(my_feature_router)\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#adding-a-new-cli-command","title":"Adding a New CLI Command","text":"<ol> <li>Create the command file <code>cortex/cli/my_cmds.py</code>:</li> </ol> <pre><code>import click\nfrom cortex.engine import CortexEngine\n\n@click.command(\"my-command\")\n@click.argument(\"project\")\n@click.option(\"--db\", default=None)\ndef my_command(project: str, db: str | None):\n    \"\"\"Description of what this command does.\"\"\"\n    engine = CortexEngine(db_path=db)\n    engine.init_db_sync()\n    # ... your logic\n    click.echo(\"Done!\")\n</code></pre> <ol> <li>Register in <code>cortex/cli/__init__.py</code>:</li> </ol> <pre><code>from cortex.cli.my_cmds import my_command\ncli.add_command(my_command)\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#adding-a-new-mcp-tool","title":"Adding a New MCP Tool","text":"<ol> <li>Add the tool in <code>cortex/mcp/server.py</code>:</li> </ol> <pre><code>@server.tool()\nasync def cortex_my_tool(param: str) -&gt; str:\n    \"\"\"Description for the AI agent.\"\"\"\n    engine = get_engine()\n    # ... your logic\n    return result\n</code></pre> <ol> <li>Add guard rules in <code>cortex/mcp/guard.py</code> if needed.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#configuration-system","title":"Configuration System","text":"<p>All config lives in <code>cortex/config.py</code>. Variables are loaded from environment at import time:</p> <pre><code>from cortex import config\n\n# Read value\nprint(config.DB_PATH)\n\n# Refresh after env changes\nconfig.reload()\n</code></pre> <p>Test Isolation</p> <p>Always call <code>config.reload()</code> after patching environment variables. The global <code>conftest.py</code> does this automatically.</p>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#writing-tests","title":"Writing Tests","text":""},{"location":"developer-guide/#test-isolation","title":"Test Isolation","text":"<p>Tests use temporary databases and <code>config.reload()</code> for isolation:</p> <pre><code>import pytest\nfrom cortex.engine import CortexEngine\n\n@pytest.fixture\ndef engine(tmp_path):\n    db = tmp_path / \"test.db\"\n    eng = CortexEngine(db_path=db, auto_embed=False)\n    eng.init_db_sync()\n    return eng\n\ndef test_store(engine):\n    fid = engine.store_sync(\"test\", \"Hello world\", fact_type=\"knowledge\")\n    assert fid &gt; 0\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#api-tests","title":"API Tests","text":"<pre><code>from fastapi.testclient import TestClient\nimport cortex.api as api_mod\n\ndef test_health(tmp_path):\n    os.environ[\"CORTEX_DB\"] = str(tmp_path / \"test.db\")\n    from cortex import config\n    config.reload()\n\n    with TestClient(api_mod.app) as c:\n        resp = c.get(\"/health\")\n        assert resp.status_code == 200\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#async-tests","title":"Async Tests","text":"<pre><code>@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_async_store(pool, engine):\n    fact_id = await engine.store(project=\"test\", content=\"async fact\")\n    assert fact_id &gt; 0\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#running-tests","title":"Running Tests","text":"<pre><code>make test          # All tests (60s timeout)\nmake test-fast     # Exclude slow tests\nmake test-slow     # Only slow tests (graph, embeddings)\nmake lint          # Ruff linter\nmake format        # Auto-format\n</code></pre>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#i18n-system","title":"i18n System","text":"<p>All user-facing error messages go through <code>cortex/i18n.py</code>:</p> <pre><code>from cortex.i18n import get_trans\n\nmsg = get_trans(\"error_not_found\", lang=\"es\")\n# \u2192 \"Recurso no encontrado\"\n</code></pre> <p>Supported languages: <code>en</code>, <code>es</code>, <code>eu</code> (Basque).</p> <p>To add a new translation: 1. Add entry to <code>TRANSLATIONS</code> dict in <code>i18n.py</code> 2. Provide <code>en</code>, <code>es</code>, and <code>eu</code> translations 3. Use <code>get_trans(\"your_key\", lang)</code> in route handlers</p>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#schema-migrations","title":"Schema Migrations","text":"<p>When adding a new table:</p> <ol> <li>Add <code>CREATE TABLE</code> to <code>cortex/schema.py</code></li> <li>Add it to the <code>ALL_SCHEMA</code> list</li> <li>Create a migration file in <code>cortex/migrations/</code> for existing databases</li> <li>Test with <code>cortex init</code> on a fresh DB and <code>cortex migrate</code> on existing</li> </ol>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#coding-conventions","title":"Coding Conventions","text":"Convention Rule Sync methods Suffix with <code>_sync</code> (e.g., <code>store_sync()</code>) Async methods No suffix (e.g., <code>store()</code>) SQL Always parameterized (<code>?</code> placeholders, never f-strings) Secrets Environment variables only, never hardcoded Logging <code>logging.getLogger(\"cortex.module_name\")</code> Dates ISO 8601 via <code>cortex.temporal.now_iso()</code> Hashing <code>cortex.canonical.canonical_json()</code> for deterministic serialization Error handling Specific exceptions (<code>sqlite3.Error</code>, <code>ValueError</code>), never bare <code>except</code> Types Full type hints, <code>py.typed</code> marker included Formatting Ruff (line length 100, target Python 3.10)"},{"location":"developer-guide/#continuous-documentation","title":"Continuous Documentation","text":"<p>CORTEX uses <code>mkdocstrings</code> for auto-generating API documentation from source code. When you add new methods, update docstrings, or modify classes, the changes will reflect without manual Markdown edits.</p>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#local-live-reload","title":"Local Live Reload","text":"<p>Run the local development server with hot-reloading:</p> <pre><code>make docs-serve\n</code></pre> <p>Any changes to <code>.md</code> files in <code>docs/</code> OR changes to Python docstrings in <code>cortex/</code> will trigger an instant rebuild and reload the browser automatically.</p>"},{"location":"developer-guide/#documenting-python-code","title":"Documenting Python Code","text":"<p>Use Google-style docstrings with type hints for optimal mkdocstrings parsing:</p> <pre><code>def my_function(param: int) -&gt; The P vs NP-Thermodynamic Bridge (Complexity \u2194 Physics)","text":"<p>Invariant: NP-Hardness is a physical phase transition, not just a time constraint.</p> <ul> <li>Phase Transitions: In hard instances of NP-Complete problems, the solution space undergoes Shattering. Do not open public GitHub issues for security vulnerabilities.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/","title":"The 5 Structurally Impossible Failures","text":"<p>\"The most valuable insight of a product rarely comes from what it does \u2014 it comes from what it guarantees cannot happen. Define the failure you make impossible and you have the most powerful sales message in your category.\"</p> <p>This document defines, with technical precision, the five failure modes that CORTEX makes structurally impossible \u2014 not merely unlikely, not hard to trigger, but architecturally excluded from the system's state space.</p> <p>The distinction matters. \"Hard to do\" is a UX promise. \"Structurally impossible\" is an engineering guarantee.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#the-analogy-static-analysis-tests","title":"The Analogy: Static Analysis Tests","text":"<p>The best mental model is a Static Analysis Test (Level 2 in the CORTEX test taxonomy):</p> <pre><code>def test_constants_are_hardcoded_not_env_injectable():\n    \"\"\"\n    Reads its own source as text and asserts structural invariants.\n    Validates STRUCTURE, not behavior.\n\n    The code can behave correctly today and be architecturally broken\n    at the same time. This test closes that gap.\n    \"\"\"\n    import inspect\n    import cortex.security.constants as m\n    source = inspect.getsource(m)\n    assert 'ENCRYPTION_VERSION = \"v6_aesgcm\"' in source\n</code></pre> <p>This test is immune to the silence interval: the period where code is already broken but behavioral tests still pass. It operates in the space of code, not execution.</p> <p>CORTEX applies the same principle to AI agent memory: it doesn't test whether the agent behaves like it remembers \u2014 it enforces a structure where not remembering is impossible.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#failure-1-session-amnesia","title":"Failure 1: Session Amnesia","text":"<p>Without CORTEX: Every session is T\u2080. The agent reconstructs context from what the LLM hallucinated, what the user re-explained, and what happens to be in the prompt window. The accumulated knowledge of months of work lives nowhere durable.</p> <p>The failure vector: <pre><code>Session N:   Decision made \u2192 stored in LLM context \u2192 session ends\nSession N+1: LLM has no memory \u2192 decision is gone \u2192 work repeats\n</code></pre></p> <p>Why CORTEX makes this impossible: The <code>context-snapshot.md</code> is the boot protocol. The agent cannot act before loading it. This is enforced at the protocol level \u2014 not a soft suggestion. If the snapshot doesn't exist, boot fails. If it's stale, it must be refreshed before any action is taken.</p> <p>There is no code path where an agent begins work without structural context.</p> <pre><code># Boot Protocol \u2014 mandatory, not optional\nstat -f \"%Sm\" -t \"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S\" ~/.cortex/context-snapshot.md\n# If stale: cd ~/cortex &amp;&amp; .venv/bin/python -m cortex.cli export\n</code></pre> <p>Guarantee class: <code>STRUCTURAL</code> \u2014 enforced by protocol, not by convention.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#failure-2-repeated-errors","title":"Failure 2: Repeated Errors","text":"<p>Without CORTEX: The architectural mistake that cost 4 hours to diagnose in January is reproduced in March. The LLM has no memory of the resolution. The learning curve resets with every conversation.</p> <p>The failure vector: <pre><code>Error E at T\u2081 \u2192 resolved \u2192 not persisted \u2192 Error E at T\u2082 \u2192 re-diagnosed\nROI of original resolution: 0%\n</code></pre></p> <p>Why CORTEX makes this impossible: The error memory layer persists root cause, resolution, and context:</p> <pre><code>cortex store --type error --source agent:gemini PROJECT \\\n  \"Root: broad except Exception in router.py:L42. \\\n   Fix: specific OSError + TimeoutError. \\\n   Pattern: all async I/O borders need targeted exception types.\"\n</code></pre> <p>Before acting on any task, the agent queries error memory for the current project. A resolved error cannot be invisible to future sessions.</p> <p>Guarantee class: <code>BEHAVIORAL</code> \u2014 enforced by protocol, probabilistically complete with <code>--type error</code> persistence discipline.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#failure-3-fabricated-history","title":"Failure 3: Fabricated History","text":"<p>Without CORTEX: The LLM invents a rationale for a decision that was never made. It fills gaps with plausible-sounding context. This is indistinguishable from true history to anyone who wasn't present.</p> <p>The most dangerous property of hallucinated history: it's coherent. It passes casual review. It only fails under adversarial scrutiny.</p> <p>The failure vector: <pre><code>Decision at T\u2081: not persisted\nQuery at T\u2082: \"Why did we choose OAuth2 PKCE?\"\nLLM response: plausible rationale, entirely fabricated\nDownstream: architecture built on fictional foundation\n</code></pre></p> <p>Why CORTEX makes this impossible: Every decision is stored with: - Content (what was decided) - Source (<code>agent:gemini</code>, <code>user:borjamoskv</code>) - Timestamp (immutable, wall-clock) - Encryption (<code>v6_aesgcm</code> \u2014 AES-256-GCM, authenticated) - Hash chain (SHA-256 linked to previous entry)</p> <p>If a decision is not in CORTEX, it did not happen. The structural ground truth cannot be fabricated because the hash chain would break.</p> <pre><code>cortex verify 42\n# \u2192 \u2705 VERIFIED \u2014 Hash chain intact, Merkle sealed\n# \u2192 \u274c TAMPERED \u2014 Hash chain broken at entry 41\n</code></pre> <p>Guarantee class: <code>CRYPTOGRAPHIC</code> \u2014 SHA-256 hash chain + Merkle checkpoints. Tamper is mathematically detectable.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#failure-4-ghost-accumulation","title":"Failure 4: Ghost Accumulation","text":"<p>Without CORTEX: Incomplete work is invisible. Each session believes it's starting from a clean state. Projects accumulate ghosts \u2014 work that was started, not completed, and then silently restarted from scratch.</p> <p>The compound cost: the time spent on the original incomplete work, plus the time spent on the restart, minus the value of the actual completion.</p> <p>The failure vector: <pre><code>T\u2081: Feature X starts\nT\u2082: Feature X interrupted (deprioritized)\nT\u2083: New session \u2014 agent unaware of X\nT\u2084: Feature X starts again from scratch\nT\u2085: Previous partial implementation found \u2014 conflict\n</code></pre></p> <p>Why CORTEX makes this impossible: The ghost taxonomy (<code>--type ghost</code>) surfaces incomplete work at boot:</p> <pre><code>cortex store --type ghost --source agent:gemini PROJECT \\\n  \"Feature X: 60% complete. Blocked: needs Y before proceeding. \\\n   Files touched: cortex/router.py, tests/test_router.py.\"\n</code></pre> <p>Ghosts are classified by count at boot. If <code>&gt; 10 total ghosts</code>: surface to operator. No ghost is invisible to a future session.</p> <p>Guarantee class: <code>STRUCTURAL</code> \u2014 enforced at boot, not discoverable during work.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#failure-5-divergence-in-multi-agent-systems","title":"Failure 5: Divergence in Multi-Agent Systems","text":"<p>Without CORTEX: Two autonomous agents operating in parallel build different versions of reality. Agent A believes decision D was made. Agent B operates on \u00acD. The conflict is silent until it reaches production \u2014 where it manifests as non-deterministic, unreproducible behavior.</p> <p>The failure vector: <pre><code>Agent A: reads context \u2192 believes \"use Redis for session cache\"\nAgent B: reads context \u2192 believes \"use Memcached for session cache\"\nMerge: conflicting implementations, undefined behavior in production\n</code></pre></p> <p>Why CORTEX makes this impossible: All agents read from the same structural ground truth: <code>~/.cortex/context-snapshot.md</code>, generated from <code>cortex.db</code>. There is no mechanism by which two agents can hold contradictory beliefs about a persisted fact \u2014 because both read the same source.</p> <p>The WBFT (Weighted Byzantine Fault-Tolerant) consensus layer additionally verifies multi-agent agreement before critical operations:</p> <pre><code># cortex/engine/wbft.py\n# Reputation-weighted voting: consensus required before write\nawait wbft_consensus.verify_quorum(agents, decision)\n</code></pre> <p>Guarantee class: <code>CONSENSUS</code> \u2014 WBFT Byzantine fault-tolerant verification.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#the-impossibility-matrix","title":"The Impossibility Matrix","text":"Failure Without CORTEX With CORTEX Guarantee Class Session Amnesia Silent, continuous Impossible <code>STRUCTURAL</code> Repeated Errors Common Impossible <code>BEHAVIORAL</code> Fabricated History Undetectable Detectable + Impossible <code>CRYPTOGRAPHIC</code> Ghost Accumulation Invisible Surfaced at boot <code>STRUCTURAL</code> Multi-Agent Divergence Silent until production Impossible <code>CONSENSUS</code>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#the-core-claim","title":"The Core Claim","text":"<p>CORTEX makes it structurally impossible for an autonomous AI agent to act on fabricated, amnesiac, or unaudited context.</p> <p>This is not a feature claim. It's an invariant \u2014 equivalent to the static analysis test that reads its own source and asserts \"this string must be here.\" If the invariant breaks, the system fails loudly, before runtime, before production, before the breach.</p>"},{"location":"impossible-failures/#for-technical-auditors","title":"For Technical Auditors","text":"<p>The impossibility guarantees are verifiable:</p> <pre><code># Verify hash chain integrity\ncortex verify --full\n\n# Run static analysis tests (L2)\npytest tests/test_l2_static_analysis.py -v\n\n# Check compliance posture\ncortex compliance-report\n# \u2192 Compliance Score: 5/5 \u2014 All Article 12 requirements met\n\n# Audit decision lineage\ncortex lineage --fact-id 42\n</code></pre> <p>All guarantees are auditable without trusting CORTEX's own claims. The hash chain is the proof.</p> <p>CORTEX v7 \u2014 Trust Infrastructure for Autonomous AI cortexpersist.com \u00b7 Apache 2.0</p>"},{"location":"installation/","title":"Installation","text":""},{"location":"installation/#requirements","title":"Requirements","text":"<ul> <li>Python 3.10 or later</li> <li>macOS, Linux, or relationship between CORTEX (Operational Memory) and NotebookLM (Documentary Consultation).</p>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#1-the-tri-layer-knowledge-stack","title":"1. The Tri-Layer Knowledge Stack","text":"<p>To prevent epistemic drift and identity divergence, the system operates on three distinct layers of knowledge:</p> Layer System Intent Nature Permissions L0: Ground Truth CORTEX Operational Reality Atomic, Encrypted, Verifiable Agent: R/W L1: Rendered Docs MkDocs Architectural Blueprint Narrative, Structured, Plaintext Agent: R (via source) L2: Dialectic Lens NotebookLM Natural Language Query Conversational, Fluid, Transient Agent: Read-Only"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#the-epistemic-imperative-verifiable-correctness","title":"The Epistemic Imperative: Verifiable Correctness","text":"<p>Axiom \u03a9\u2083-V: El fallo m\u00e1s peligroso de una IA no es la incorrecci\u00f3n, sino la correcci\u00f3n no verificable.</p> <p>Maintaining the writing layer (CORTEX) cryptographically isolated from the synthesis layer (NotebookLM) ensures that every \"insight\" generated at L2 has an immutable audit trail leading back to L0 Ground Truth.</p> <ul> <li>Insight without Audit = Hallucination.</li> <li>Audit without Ground Truth = Calcification.</li> <li>Isolation = Sovereignty.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#rule-zero-cortex-writes-notebooklm-reads","title":"Rule Zero: CORTEX Writes, NotebookLM Reads","text":"<p>Agents must never attempt to persist decisions, facts, or state changes directly into NotebookLM. NotebookLM is a temporary lens for reasoning over documents, not a source of truth. If a contradiction arises between NotebookLM's output and a CORTEX fact, CORTEX is always correct.</p>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#2-operational-protocol","title":"2. Operational Protocol","text":""},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#21-the-export-pipeline","title":"2.1 The Export Pipeline","text":"<p>Data flows one-way: <code>CORTEX \u2192 Markdown Docs \u2192 NotebookLM</code>.</p> <ol> <li>CORTEX stores the atomic reality (decisions, ghosts, bridges).</li> <li>CLI/Exporter transforms encrypted/atomic data into human-readable Markdown snapshots.</li> <li>NotebookLM ingests these snapshots as sources.</li> </ol>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#22-staleness-and-naming-convention","title":"2.2 Staleness and Naming Convention","text":"<p>To prevent reasoning over outdated data, all sources in NotebookLM must follow the mandatory naming convention:</p> <p><code>cortex-{domain}-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md</code></p> <ul> <li>Domain: Specific project or semantic cluster (e.g., <code>agentica</code>, <code>sap-audit</code>, <code>global</code>).</li> <li>Date: The exact day the snapshot was taken.</li> <li>Protocol: Any source older than 48 hours is considered \"High Risk\" for operational decisions.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#23-the-encryption-boundary","title":"2.3 The Encryption Boundary","text":"<ul> <li>Encrypted facts (v6 AES-GCM): Must remain inside CORTEX. They are never exported to NotebookLM.</li> <li>Architecture &amp; Axioms: Public/plaintext documentation is eligible for NotebookLM ingestion.</li> <li>Sensitive Data: Avoid uploading files containing PII, credentials, or financial data to personal NotebookLM accounts. Use Workspace Enterprise accounts for sensitive architectural reasoning.</li> </ul>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#3-risks-and-mitigations","title":"3. Risks and Mitigations","text":""},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#31-dual-embedding-divergence","title":"3.1 Dual-Embedding Divergence","text":"<p>The system uses two different semantic spaces:</p> <ol> <li>CORTEX: <code>MiniLM-L6-v2</code> (384-dim) with Read-as-Rewrite topology mutation.</li> <li>NotebookLM: Proprietary Google Gemini embeddings.</li> </ol> <p>Risk: The same query might yield different relevant context in each system. Mitigation: Use NotebookLM for synthesis and discovery, but use CORTEX <code>recall</code> for execution and validation.</p>"},{"location":"knowledge-architecture/#32-confused-agency","title":"3.2 Confused Agency","text":"<p>Risk: An agent believes NotebookLM is its primary memory and stops checking CORTEX. Pero el verdadero destructor de valor es cuadr\u00e1tico (O(n\u00b2)): decisiones err\u00f3neas que llegan a producci\u00f3n y generan cascadas de rework.</p> <p>El mecanismo de cascada:</p> <ol> <li>Sin <code>fact_type: decision</code>, el agente re-toma una decisi\u00f3n arquitect\u00f3nica incorrectamente.</li> <li>El error no se detecta inmediatamente \u2014 se propaga a N archivos dependientes.</li> <li>La detecci\u00f3n ocurre d\u00edas/semanas despu\u00e9s, cuando el coste de rework es N\u00b2 (cada archivo afectado interact\u00faa con otros archivos afectados).</li> </ol> Par\u00e1metro Valor Justificaci\u00f3n Decisiones catastr\u00f3ficas evitadas/a\u00f1o ~15 Estimaci\u00f3n conservadora: 1 cada ~15 d\u00edas laborables Rework medio por decisi\u00f3n cascada 60 h Rango real: 40-80h seg\u00fan profundidad de la cascada Coste/hora (Staff/Senior, media) \u20ac85/h Peso ponderado entre perfiles que detectan y reparan Coste evitado por cascadas \u20ac76,500/a\u00f1o 15 \u00d7 60h \u00d7 \u20ac85/h <p>ROI revisado:</p> Componente Ahorro anual Ahorro lineal (\u00a74.2) \u2014 tiempo de redundancia \u20ac29,407 Ahorro cuadr\u00e1tico (\u00a74.6) \u2014 cascadas evitadas \u20ac76,500 Total corregido \u20ac105,907 <p>Para equipos de 5 seniors, el ahorro total corregido escala a ~\u20ac530K/a\u00f1o \u2014 equivalente a 3-4 contrataciones completas evitadas.</p> <p>Principio epist\u00e9mico: El tiempo de reconstruir contexto es O(1) y predecible. Un error repetido que llega a producci\u00f3n es O(n\u00b2) porque genera cascadas. CORTEX no ahorra 588 horas lineales \u2014 previene las ~15 decisiones catastr\u00f3ficas/a\u00f1o que cada una cuesta 40-80h de rework.</p>"},{"location":"memory_architecture_and_roi/#5-conclusion","title":"5. Conclusi\u00f3n","text":"<p>La memoria nativa de Antigravity es suficiente para tareas aisladas y puntuales. CORTEX transforma al agente de un operador con amnesia anter\u00f3grada en un sistema con memoria continua, integridad criptogr\u00e1fica, y transferencia de conocimiento entre proyectos.</p> <p>El ahorro anual conservador es de ~\u20ac29K/developer en tiempo lineal. Pero el ahorro real, incluyendo cascadas de decisiones err\u00f3neas evitadas, se acerca a \u20ac106K-\u20ac130K/developer (o \u20ac530K+ por equipo de 5). La diferencia entre ambas cifras es exactamente el coste de la entrop\u00eda que no se midi\u00f3.</p> <p>Documento generado: 2026-02-24 \u00b7 CORTEX v12.2 \u00b7 Apache 2.0</p>"},{"location":"omega_handoff_roadmap_q1/","title":"\u03a9\u03a9-HANDOFF: V\u00cdA DE ESCALA (SEMANAS 1-12)","text":"<p>A continuaci\u00f3n se detalla el roadmap y los milestones para el
